title: 第十四講
en: LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. "What should we toe without Language ?

AFTER we have finished our survey of the languages which are spoken at present over the civilised world, and which have been spoken there so long as we know anything of the presence of the human race on this planet of ours, it is time to ask the question, what language really is.

Now I ask, Do you know anything in the whole world more wonderful than language 1 No doubt, even if we were not able to speak, we should still be able to see, to hear, to taste, to smell, and to feel.

We could taste what is sweet and like it, and taste what is bitter and dislike it. We might run away from the fire, because it burns, and turn towards the water, because it is cool, or because it quenches our thirst ; but we should have no words to distinguish lire from water, or hot from cold, or sweet from bitter. We should be like children who have burnt their fingers and cry, who have tasted sugar and smile, who have swallowed vinegar and howl. Some people might call this running away from what hurts, and turning towards what is pleasant, rational, just as they say that a dog is rational because he runs away from his master when he raises his stick, and jumps up at him when he holds out a piece of meat.

If by a bold metaphor this is to be called reason, we need not object., if only we distinguish between conscious and unconscious, between worded and uncorded reason, and if we remember, that, by using reason in that very enlarged sense, we may be driven in the end to call even the shutting of our eyes at the approach of a blow an act of reasoning.

However, with or without language, we might certainly do all this, and a great deal more. We might fight and kill, we might love and protect. We might, if we were very clever, accumulate dispositions and habits which by repeated inheritance would enable our descendants to build nests, or warrens, or beehive huts. The strongest might possibly learn to act as sentinels and make themselves obeyed ; the weaker sex might even invent signals of danger and other signs of communication.

I doubt not that chivalrous and unchivalrous feelings also might be aroused in our breast, such as we see among the higher animals, and that jealousy and revenge as well as friendship and love might influence our actions.

But with all this, imagine that we were sitting here, looking at one another with a kind of goodnatured bovine stare, but without a single word, not only on our lips, but in our minds ; our mind being in fact a mere negative plate, without our being able to lay hold of any of the outlines drawn on it, by saying this is this, and this is that !
Definition of Thinking1.

Some philosophers, as you know, hold that men, like animals, though they possessed no language, LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 353 might still sit silent and think. Unfortunately they do not tell us what they mean and what they do not mean by thinking, but it seems clear that they use thinking as synonymous with every kind of mental activity. Des Cartes., when discussing his fundamental principle, Cogito ergo turn, did the same; but, as an honest philosopher, he warned us that he used cogitare in that widest sense l, so as to include sensation, perception, memory, imagination, and all the rest. If the meaning of to think is avowedly stretched to that extent, no one would drearn of denying that animals, though speechless, can think, and that we also could think without language, that is to say, without ever having possessed language, without knowing one word from another.
What are we thinking- of?

But now let us ask those philosophers the simple question, If we can think without language, what are we thinking of? What indeed ? I do not wish to lay a trap, like a cross-examining lawyer. Of course, if you told me what you were thinking of, you could do it only by using a word. Nor do I claim to be a thought-reader, and tell you. without your having told me, what you are thinking off for that, of course, I could only do by using a word. But I ask you to ask yourselves, what you are thinking of, if you are thinking of anything, and I shall join myself in that experiment. Suppose we wTere all thinking, as we call it, of a dog, then as soon as we attempt to answer to ourselves the question, What are we think- qu'une chose qui pense? ("est une ohose qui doute, qui entend, qui concoit, qui affirme, qui nie, qui veut, qui no veut pas, qui imagine aussi, ct qui sent.'
A a 354 LECTURE XIV.

ing of? we can only do it by saying to ourselves or to others, Dog. It is perfectly true that canis, chien, Hund would do as well, and we need not even pronounce any of these words while remembering a certain dog, or while hearing the barking of a number of dogs about us. But though we may suppress the sound or recollection of a word after we have once heard it, or replace it even by another word taken from another language, we cannot possibly become conscious to ourselves of what we are thinking, without having the word in reserve, or, as the Italians say, in petto, or, as some savages say, in the stomach.
Thinking1 in German or English.

If any doubt still remains in your mind on the impossibility of real thought without language, ask yourselves what you mean in asking a foreigner who has long lived in England, whether he thinks in German or in English ? What would you say, if he were to answer, In neither. You would, I believe, think, and think rightly, that he was a fool.
Why we cannot think without words.

But if that is so, if thought, in the properly restricted sense of that word, is impossible without language, you may well ask, why that should be so. Many people suppose that we first form our thoughts, or as they call it our ideas, and that afterwards we go in search of certain sounds, which we attach to our ideas, and which we retain because we find them very useful for the purpose of communication. Now I ask you, is such a process possible or conceivable 1 Do Ave ever find ourselves in possession of a concept, but without a name for it, unless indeed we have for- LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 355 gotten, and know that we have forgotten, the name which we formerly possessed ? Or is there anywhere in the whole world a place where we could find empty sounds, such as father and mother, meaning nothing as yet, but ready for use when wanted ? I know some people speak of inexpressible thoughts, but they mean feelings ; others say they may have a clear concept of a plant, without knowing its name ; they speak of that plant, Oh, what do you call it ? But is not plant a name, is not vegetable a name, is not object a name, is not it a name, is not even What do you call it, a name ?

We often do not know the exact or right name, but in that case we always know the more general name. If we had never seen or heard of an elephant, we should not know its name, but we should know that it was an animal and call it so ; we should know that it was a quadruped, and call it so. If we did not know whether what we saw was an inanimate lump, or a plant, or a bird, fish, or mammal, we should have no name for it beyond the name thing. We could not name it further, because we know no more about it, because we could not bring it under any more definite conceptual name. We may see, hear, and touch the elephant, we may have a more or less exact image of it, but until we can predicate or name some distinguishing feature of it, we could neither name nor know it, in the true sense of that word.

To suppose, as is done by most philosophers, that we first find ourselves in command of an army of naked concepts, and that we afterwards array them in verbal uniforms, is impossible for two very simple reasons ; first, because there is no magazine which A a 2 3o6 LECTURE XIV.

could supply these verbal uniforms, and secondly, because we never meet with naked concepts ; or, to put it more strongly still, because we never meet with a rabbit without a skin, or an oyster without a shell.

The reason why real thought is impossible without language is very simple. What we call language is not, as is commonly supposed, thought plus sound, but what we call thought is really language minus sound. That is to say, when we are once in possession of language, we may hum our words, or remember them in perfect silence, as we remember a piece of music without a single vibration of our vocal chords. We may also abbreviate our words, so that such expressions as, ' If Plato is right,' may stand for a whole library. We may in fact eliminate the meaning of the word so that the word only remains as a symbol 1 ; we may even substitute algebraic signs for real words, and thus carry on processes of reckoning or reasoning which in their final results are perfectly astonishing. But as little as we can reckon without actual or disguised numerals, can we reason without actual or. disguised words. This is the last result to which the Science of Language has led us, and which has changed the Science of Language into the Science of Thought. ' We think in words3 must become the charter of all exact philosophy in future, and it will form, I believe, at the same time the reconciliation of all systems of philosophy in the past.
Communication, not language.
But surely, it is said, men communicate, and animals too communicate, without language. Yes, 1 Science of Thought, p. 35.

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 357 they certainly do, we all do, some more, others less successfully. The Polynesians, as Chamisso 7 tells us in his charming Voyage round the world (1815-1818), are sparing of words, and a wink often takes the place of a long speech. Perhaps it does so even among less savage races. They do not even say Yes, when they can help it, but only move their brow. It is only to a stranger that they will say Inga, yes. But such communication is not thought, if we use our words properly.

I go even a step further, and maintain that we are so made that, whether we like it or not, we must show by outward signs what passes within us. There are few people who can so repress their emotions as not to let others see when they are angry or happy. We blush, we tremble, we frown, we pout, we grin, we laugh, we smile, and what can be more tell-tale, and sometimes more eloquent, than these involuntary signs? I have no doubt that animals betray their feelings by similar signs, and that these signs are understood by their fellow-creatures. You have only to disturb an ant-hill, and see what happens. A number of ants will run away on their beaten tracks, they will stop every ant they meet, and every ant, after having been touched and communicated with, will run to the ant-hill to render help with the same alacrity with which a member of the fire-brigade runs towards the place of conflagration after hearing the bugle in the street. We cannot understand how it is done, but that little head of an ant, not larger than the head of a pin, must have been able to express terror and implore help, even as a dog will run up to 1 Chamisso's Werke, vol. i. p. 357.

you and express in his face terror, and by his motions implore your help. But when will people learn that emotions are not thoughts, and that if we call anger or joy thought, we simply muddle our own thoughts and confound our own language?

I believe that some of these involuntary manifestations of our feelings may in time lead to intentional gestures ; and we know from pantomimes, also from communications that are said to take place in America and Australia between tribes speaking different languages, that this gesture-language may be brought to a very high degree of perfection. But we must not forget that in all cases where this communication by means of gestures has been observed, the parties concerned are each in possession of a real language, that in fact they think first in their own conceptual language and then translate their thoughts back into pantomime \ The subject, however, is curious, and deserves more study than it has hitherto received. We imagine we can understand why a person kneeling down is supposed to implore mercy, why another shaking his fist is supposed to say, Stand off! But these gestures, as used in different countries, have not always the same meaning, and even the expressive 1 In the island of Gomera, one of the islands of the Canary Archipelago, people communicate by means of a whistling language. The island is traversed by many deep ravines and gullies which run out in all directions from the central plateau. They are not bridged, and can often only be crossed with great difficulty, so that people who really live very near to each other in a straight line have to make a circuit of hours when they wish to meet. Whistling has therefore become an excellent means of communication, and has gradually assumed the proportions of a true substitute for speech. But what they whistle is their own language.
LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 359 signs used by deaf and dumb people are by no means identical all over the globe 1.

Children again, long before they are able to speak, can imitate the acts of eating, drinking, riding on their father's knees, and thus express their little wishes ; but a wish is not a thought, as little as fear and horror. If some philosophers like to call these states of feeling thought, they may do so at their own peril, but they ought at all events to let us know, in order that others may be able to discount such license.
Images.

Some more serious philosophers put in a claim for images. Images, they say, such as our senses leave in our memory, may surely be called thought. They may, no doubt, if only we let others know that in our own philosophical dialect we use thinking in that extended sense. But it is surely better to distinguish and to keep the term imagination for signifying the play of our images. I myself hold it impossible that human beings should have real images without first having framed them in names ; and among physiologists, Helmholtz denies the possibility of our having perceptions without names. But, of course, if careful observers, such as Mr. Galton, assure us that they have images without knowing what they are images of, and without remembering what they are called, we are bound to believe them, even though we cannot follow them. What they are anxious for is evidently to show that animals, though they have no language, have images, that they combine these images, and that their acts, their sensible, or, as they like to call 1 Mallery, Sign Language among the North- American Indians.

them, their rational acts, are determined by them. Let that be so, at least for argument's sake. But even then, is not this imagination or even this reasoning without language utterly different from imagination and reasoning with language** Suppose a dog, instead of coming to me, as one of my dogs did, expressing his uneasiness and then dragging me on to his rug which was red, and showing me that it was occupied by my other dog, who ought to have been on his own rug which was blue, looking at me reproachfully till I had ordered the other dog away, and then taking possession with all the pride of an injured innocent of his own red rug — suppose that dog, instead of wheedling and barking were suddenly to stand up on his hind legs and say to me, ' The other dog has taken my rug ; please, Sir, order him away,' should we not almost go out of our mind 1 Or let us place an infant and a grown-up man side by side, the one struggling and crying for a cup of milk, the other saying plainly, ' I should like that cup of milk.' Is not the distance between these two acts immeasurable, the one being merely the result of the direct or reflex action of our senses, the other the result of a growth that has gone on for thousands of years ? The grown-up man also, if he were dying of thirst, might no doubt rush towards the cup and swallow it without saying a word, and we might call the expression of his impetuous features language, and his rushing movements reason. But we should gain nothing by the use of this metaphorical language. There are philosophers who tell us that an infant could not stretch out its arms without £oin£ through a silent syllogism : ' By stretching out our arms we LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 361 obtain wheat we wish for ; I wish for this cup of milk, therefore I stretch out my arms.' It may be so, but we know nothing about it and never shall, till the infant is able to speak, that is to say, ceases to be an infant, and then tells us what it thinks.

Between the infant, however, and the man who is able to speak, there is not a distance of ten or twenty years only. The language which he has accepted is the result of intellectual labour carried on for thousands of years. The original framing of our words and thoughts is a process which no one but the geologist of language has even the most remote idea of, and to suppose that one human being could, in the space of ten or twenty years, have accumulated the wealth of his grammar and dictionary is like believing that the earth with its mountains and rivers could have been made in six days. It is extraordinary that the same argument, which has been answered ad nauseam, is brought forward again and again. It is quite true that the infant and the parrot are for a time without language, and that both learn to say after a time, 'How do you do?' But the child learns to speak human language, while the parrot never speaks Parrot ese.
Involuntary and voluntary sounds.

The next step after what has been called the language of gestures, leads us on to involuntary and voluntary sounds. I call involuntary sounds interjections which have a direct natural origin, which express joy, fear, anger, admiration, assent or dissent. To us, accustomed to our own interjections, there seems a natural appropriateness in their sound, but here too a comparative study teaches us that it is not 362 LECTURE XIV.

so. No, for instance, does not always mean no ; in Syrianian it means yes. Even in Irish we find for No, not only naicc, but also aicc 1. Voluntary interjections I call such imitative sounds as how wow for dog, and moo for cow. Here, too, we find that what seems to us perfectly natural and intelligible, is not always so. Whereas to our ears the dog says bow wow, he says Miff klaff to a German ear.

It is extremely difficult to render inarticulate sounds by our alphabet. Many attempts have been made to write down the sounds uttered by birds, but hitherto with small success. A great phonetician, well acquainted with the latest theories of physiological phonetics, has spent many days and nights in watching the notes of the nightingale ; and what do you think his rendering has come to 1 The real note of the nightingale, as reduced to alphabetical writing, is :
Dailidurei faledirannurei lidundei faledaritturei.

You know that before languages were studied scientifically, it was a very general idea that all human speech arose in that way, and that the ultimate elements of our words were imitations of natural sounds or involuntary interjections. I called these theories the Bow-wow and Pooli-pooh theories. Some philosophers have lately added a third theory, which they call the Yo-heho theory, but which is really a subdivision only of the Pooli-pooli theory. By a most extraordinary mistake this theory has been ascribed to Noire, who was really one of its most determined opponents. According to this theory language would have been derived directly from the 1 Zeuss, Grammatica Celtica. Yes in Old Irish is iss ed, ' est hoc,' or simply ed, =€k>th. ita. — Whitley Stokes.
LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 363 cries uttered by people while engaged in pulling, rubbing, digging, rowing, and similar primitive occupations.

In this the supporters of this Yo-heho theory have, no doubt, touched on a very important phase in the growth of language and thought, as we shall see presently ; but if they look upon sounds such as Yo-heho as mere interjections, they are still in the bitterness of the Pooh-pooh theory, that is to say, they have not even perceived the difficulty of the problem which they wish to solve.

The names Bow-wow, Pooh-pooh, and Yo-heho theories have sometimes been objected to as too homely, and as possibly offensive. But as these theories in their crude form are no longer held by any scholar, these names are really quite harmless, and they are certainly useful, because they tell their own tale. If we are afraid of them, we must use the cumbersome names of Mimetic, Onomatojioetic or Interject ional theory, every one of them requiring an elaborate commentary.
The Bow-wow, Pooh-pooh, and Yo-heho theories.

These three theories, however, were by no means so illogical as they seem to us now. They were no doubt a ijriori theories, but they had certain facts to support them. There are interjections in every language, and, by the general analogy of language, some of them have been raised into verbs and adjectives and substantives. Hush, for instance, the German husch, is an interjection which in German is used to drive away birds, to express any quick movement; to attract attention, while in English it is now chiefly 364 LECTURE XIV.

employed to enjoin silence. From this interjection, and from no root, are derived in German the adjective husch, meaning quick, and the substantive Husch, quickness, also a blow, a box on the ear. Thus the lines in Shakespeare's Hamlet, And we have done but greenly In hugger mugger to inter him.
are translated in German by Und thoricht war's von tins, so unter'm Husch Ihn zu bestatten.

We have besides a German feminine substantive, Die Husche, which means a shower of rain, and two verbs, huschen, to move quickly, and husch eln, to scamp one's work. In English to hush has taken the exclusive meaning of to enjoin silence, to quiet.
This would be an illustration of the Pooh-pooh theory.

The Boiv-woiv theory can claim a number of words, the best known being cuckoo, in Greek kokkv£, in Latin cucMus, in Sanskrit kokila. In Greek we have also a verb kokkv(€lv, redupl. perfect, k€k6kkvkcl, to cry cuckoo.

The Yo-heho theory is really a subdivision of the Pooh-pooh theory, but it may be illustrated by bang ! as an interjection that accompanies a blow; to bang, to beat violently, and banged hair, which has lately been much admired.

It would be a most interesting subject to collect all the words which, whether in English or in German, or in Latin, Greek and Sanskrit, are formed direct from interjectional elements. And it would teach us better than anything else that, after we have claimed all that can rightly be claimed for this LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 365 amorphous stratum of human speech, we have only taken the mere outworks, while the real fortress of language has not been touched.
Roots.

That fortress could not be taken by storm, but only by a regular siege ; — it will not surrender to a priori arguments, but only to a 'posteriori analysis. This analysis was carried out by the founders of Comparative Philology, by Bopp, Grimm, Pott and others ; but it had been attempted more than two thousand years ago by Sanskrit grammarians. They had taken Sanskrit, one of the richest and most primitive of Aryan languages, and by submitting every word of it to a careful analysis, that is to say, by separating all that could be separated and proved to be merely formal, they had succeeded in discovering certain elements which would yield to no further analysis, and which they therefore treated as the ultimate facts of language, and designated as roots.

The number of roots admitted by these ancient Sanskrit grammarians was far too large however. We have now reduced their number to about 800, — I believe they will be reduced still further, — and with these we undertake to account for all the really important words which occur in Sanskrit literature. In more modern languages many clusters of words derived from one root have become extinct, and their place is taken by secondary and tertiary derivatives of other roots, so that for the English Dictionary (now being published at Oxford), which is said to comprehend 250,000 words, no more than about 460 roots '
1 Science of Thought, p. 210.

are required to account for all that has been said by Shakespeare, Milton, and Byron. But more than that : the number of independent concepts conveyed by these 800 Sanskrit roots, is not 800, or anything like it, but has been reduced to the small number of 121. With these 121 radical concepts every thought that has ever passed through a human brain can be. and has been expressed. This would have sounded like a wild dream to Plato and Aristotle, nay even to Locke and Kant, and yet it is a fact that can no more be questioned than the fact that the whole kaleidoscope of nature — all that was ever seen in this myriad-shaped world of ours — is made up of about sixty elementary substances.

With regard to the meaning of the 800 roots of Sanskrit, we find that most of them express acts, such as striking, digging, rubbing, crushing, pounding, cutting, gathering, mixing, sprinkling, burning, — acts in fact which represent some of the primitive occupations of man, but which by means of generalisation, specialisation, and metaphor have been made to express the most abstract ideas of our advanced society. A root meaning to strike supplied names for a good stroke of business and for striking remarks. To dig came to mean to search for and to inquire. To rub was used for rubbing down, softening, appeasing; to burn came to mean to love, and also to be ashamed ; and to gather did excellent service for expressing in primitive logic what we now call observation of facts, the connection of major and minor, or even syllogism.
And now we must gather up the threads of our own argument.

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 367 We saw that real thought was impossible without words. We have now seen that all words are made of roots, and that these roots expressed originally primitive co-operative acts, such as would be performed by men in the earliest stages of their social life.
Words derived from conceptual roots.

But this is not all. Let us remember that what shook for the first time the faith of those who thought they could explain all the words of our language as imitations of natural sounds was the strange fact that in the names of animals there was no trace whatever of these sounds. After the cuckoo had been claimed as a case in point, as the great trophy or totem of the Bow-wow theory, everything else collapsed. In the names for dog there was no trace of bow-wow, in the names for horse no trace of neighing, in the names for donkey no trace of braying, in the names for cow no trace of mooing. On the contrary, it was found that every word which was derived from a root expressed a general concept. The name for horse, the Latin equus, the Sanskrit asva, was derived from a root meaning to be sharp or quick ; hence it became clear that the horse had been conceived and named as a runner or racer. From the same root came words for stone, spear, needle, point, sharpness of sight, quickness of thought, to the very 'cuteness of the New World.
The serpent was called from a root meaning to creep along, and another name of it, the Sk. a hi, the Greek e^u, came from a root meaning to throttle.
Sun, Gothic siinna, is derived from a root su, to bring forth ; son, Gothic sunns, comes from the same 368 LECTUKE XIV.
root, in a passive sense, and meant originally the begotten, filius.
Hand comes from a root which exists in Gothic as hinthan, to seize.

Eye, Gothic augo, Lat. oc-ulus, Sk. aksha, all come from the same root, which meant originally to point, to pierce. Another name for eye in Sanskrit is ne train, which means the leader, from ni, to lead.

So we could go on for ever tracing back every word to its root and its radical concept. I do not mean to say that we succeed in every case. There are still many words which have not been brought to disclose their secret history, and there is still plenty of work to do for critical etymologists.

There are many words which require no knowledge of Sanskrit at all for their etymological explanation, and which we use constantly without thinking of their etymological meaning. Thus a settle is clearly what we sit on, and so also, though less directly, a saddle ; a road is what we ride on; a stand what we stand on ; a bier is what bears us, a burden what we bear ourselves ; a shaft is what is shaved or planed ; a draft what is drawn, a drift what is driven, a rift what is riven. A thrill of joy, or a thrilling story, both come from to thrill, to pierce, to perforate : but to bore also, whatever its historical origin may have been, is now used to express that slow rotatory worrying talk which is apt to make us gnash our teeth \ Well then, you may take it as an established fact that, with the exception of some onomatopoetic survivals, our words are in the main conceptual ; that 1 On the introduction of the word bore, see Academy, Jan. 5, 12, LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 369 they are derived from conceptual roots, or, to put it differently, that our words are concepts. If therefore it is admitted that we cannot think except in concepts, it will be easy enough to understand why we cannot think except in words.
Are concepts possible without words ?

But you may say, Cannot a concept exist without a word 1 Certainly not, though in order to meet every possible objection, we may say that no concept can exist without a sign, whether it be a word or anything else. And if it is asked, whether the concept exists first, and the sign comes afterwards, I should say No ; the two are simultaneous : but in strict logic, the sign, being the condition of a concept, may really be said to come first. After a time, words may be dropt, and it is then, when we try to remember the old word that gave birth to our concept, that we are led to imagine that concepts come first, and words afterwards.
Berkeley.

I know from my own experience how difficult it is to see this clearly. We are so accustomed to think without words, that is to say, after having dropt our words, that we can hardly realise the fact that originally no conceptual thought was possible without these or other signs. No strong man, unless he was told, would believe that originally he could not walk without leading-strings. Berkeley seems to have struggled all his ]ife with this problem, and honest as he always is, he gives us the most contradictory conclusions at which he arrived from time to time. It was one of the fundamental principles of his philosophy that Eb 370 LECTURE XIV.

concepts, or what were then called general ideas, are impossible except by attaching a word or sign to a percept, or what he called a particular idea. Hence he knew that concepts were impossible without words, and discursive thought impossible without concepts. But in spite of that he was often very angry with these words, and in the Introduction to his ' Treatise concerning the Principles of Human Understanding ' (1710) he wrote: 'Since therefore words are so apt to impose on the understanding [I am resolved in my enquiries to make as little use of them as possibly I can] : whatever ideas I consider I shall endeavour to take them bare and naked into my view, keeping out of my thoughts, so far as I am able, those names which long and constant use have so strictly united with them V Again, in his Common-place Book (Works, ed. Fraser, vol. i. p. 1 52), he says : ' If men would lay aside words in thinking, 'tis impossible they should ever mistake, save only in matters of fact. I mean it seems impossible they should be positive and secure that anything was true which in truth is not so. Certainly I cannot err in matter of simple perception. So far as we can in reasoning go without the help of signs, there we have certain knowledge. Indeed, in long deductions made by signs there may be slips of memory.' Having thus delivered his soul against words — the very signs without which concepts, as he shows, were impossible, or which were at all events strictly united with our thoughts — he breaks forth in another place (vol. iv. p. 455) in the following 1 The Irish bull, enclosed in brackets, was omitted in the second edition.

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 371 panegyric : ' Words (by them meaning all sorts of signs) are so necessary, instead of being (when duly used or in their own nature) prejudicial to the advancement of knowledge, or an hindrance to knowledge, that without them there could in mathematiques themselves be no demonstration.'

It seems to me that most modern philosophers are just in the same state of haziness with regard to the relation between thought and language as Berkeley was ; only they are not quite so honest towards themselves. The Bishop, for instance, in another passage of his Common-place Book (vol. iv. p. 429), after having satisfied himself ' that it would be absurd to use words for recording our thoughts to ourselves or in some private meditations,' interpellates himself by adding the following note, 'Is discursive thought, then, independent of language ? ' He forgot that he had given the answer himself, namely, that it was not and that it could not be.
Process of naming".

Suppose we see the same colour in snow, milk, chalk, and linen. We cannot single it out, take it away or abstract it from the different sensuous objects in which it occurs, unless we have a sign or handle to do it with, and that sign, for all the ordinary purposes of thinking, is a word, such as white. Until that word is there, we may have different sensations, but no concepts, not even percepts, in the true sense of the word. It is the electric spark of the word which changes something common to various sensations into a percept, as, afterwards, it changes something common to various percepts into a concept, b b 2 372 LECTURE XIV.
and something common to various concepts into a higher concept.

But whence came that electric spark ] Where did men find that sign to signify many things ; and did not that sign already, in order to be applicable to different perceptions, require something of a comprehensive or conceptual character ?
Origin of concepts.

Yes, it did. And here lies the punctum sal i ens of the whole philosophy of language. Long before the question was asked, how man came in possession of words, there was the old question, how man came in possession of concepts. Nearly all philosophers drew the line of demarcation between man and beast at concepts. Up to concepts the two seemed alike.
Then the question arose, How did man alone go beyond percepts and arrive at concepts ?

The usual answer was that man possessed some peculiar gift or faculty which enabled him to form concepts, and to comprehend the manifold as one. Even now many philosophers are satisfied with that mythology. But this answer is no answer at all. We might as well say that man began to write because he had the faculty of writing. We want to know what forced man to form concepts, whether he liked it or not. Why should he not have been satisfied with what the senses gave him, with seeing this, with hearing that ? WThy should he have gone beyond the single images and looked for the general ? He might have been very happy in the world of sensations, perceptions, and images. Why should he ever have left it ?

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 373 What we call the roots of language betray the secret. Almost all of them express, as we saw, the common acts of man. Now before man is conscious as yet of any object, as an object, he cannot help being conscious of his own acts, and as these acts are mostly repeated and continuous acts, he becomes conscious, without any new effort, of his many or repeated acts as one. Here lies the genesis of the most primitive and, I may add, the first inevitable concepts : they consist in our consciousness of our own repeated acts as one continuous action. To rub, for instance, was not only to rub once, and then again, and then again, but it was the continuous act of rubbing, afterwards of smoothing, softening, appeasing; and thus the root, meaning originally to rub, came in time to mean to appease the anger of the gods. There is an uninterrupted chain or development between our saying, Oh God, have mercy ! and our earliest ancestors' saying, Be rubbed down, be smooth, be softened, ye gods !
Former theories.

It will now perhaps become clear why the three old theories of the origin of language and thought, the Pooh-pooh theory, the Boiu-ivotv theory, and the Yo-keho theory, completely fail to explain what has to be explained, namely, how conceptual words arose. Cuckoo would be an imitation of the sound of the cuckoo, bow-wow of the barking of the dog, poohpooh of our contempt, yo-heho of our labour ; but with all this we should never get out of the enchanted circle of mere sensuous knowledge. We want conceptual sounds. How can we get them ?

Now here the advantage of what I shall call in future the Synergastic theory will at once become evident. If, as we know, people in a primitive state accompany most of their common acts by sounds, then the clamor concomitans of these acts is not the sign of a single act, but the inseparable accompaniment of our consciousness of our many repeated acts as one action. Here we see the first dawn of conceptual thought. If this is once clearly perceived, it will likewise be perceived that the difference between this theory of the origin of conceptual language and the old onomatopoetic theories is not one of degree, but of kind, and marks a greater advance in the Science of Language than the Copernican theory did in the Science of Astronomy. Here lies Noire" s real merit. He was the first who saw that the natural genesis of concepts was to be found in the consciousness of our acts. I was able to give the proof of it by showing that nearly all roots in Sanskrit were expressive of our acts. Those who do not see the difficulties which have to be explained when we ask for the origin of our conceptual roots, may consider the old Pooh-pooh and Bow-wow theories quite sufficient. To the true philosopher the Synergastic theory is the only one which approaches or touches the hem of the problem that has to be solved, namely, how concepts arose, and how concepts were expressed.
The ' clamor concomitans.'

One question only we are unable to answer, namely, why the clamor concomitans of the different acts of men, the consciousness of which constituted their first concepts, should have been exactly what it was. Why LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 375 in crushing they should have uttered MAR, in carrying VAH, in stretching TAN, in scattering STAR, is beyond our ken. All we can say is that the possibilities in uttering and still more in fixing these sounds were almost unlimited, and that though we may imagine that we perceive some reasonableness in some of them, we very soon come to the end of such speculations.

Who does not imagine that there is some similarity between the root VA, to blow, and the sound of our own breathing, or, if we adopt the mimetic theory, the sound of the wind? But if that is so with VA, what shall we say to DHAM, to blow, and /SVAS, to breathe? That there should be in some cases some vague similarity between the sound of a root and the sound produced by the work which it accompanies is intelligible, and so far the speculations on the supposed inherent meaning of certain letters, which begin as early as Plato's Cratylos, are not without some value. Possibly, if we could go back to an earlier stage in the formation of roots, his speculations might seem still better founded. But we must here too learn to be satisfied with what is within the reach of historical knowledge, or, if we must needs stretch our powers of vision beyond, follow the example of Plato and not assume too serious a countenance.
A few quotations from Plato will serve to make my meaning clear.
' Now the letter R,' he says \ ' appeared to the imposer of names an excellent instrument for the expression of motion ; and he frequently used the letter 1 Cratylos, p. 426.

for this purpose : for example, in the actual words pziv and por\ he represents motion by r; also in the words Tponos, trembling, rpayvs, rugged ; and again, in words such as dpaveiv, to crush, Kpovuv, to strike, €p€LK€iv, to bruise, dpvTTTeu', to break, Kepiiari^iv, to crumble, pvpLfietv, to whirl : of all the sorts of movement he generally finds an expression in the letter R, because, as I imagine, he had observed that the tongue was most agitated and least at rest in the pronunciation of this letter, which he therefore used in order to express motion.'

Let us consider these remarks for one moment. Nothing would be easier than to produce an equal number of words in which r occurs, and which express not motion, but rest ; for instance, paxis, rib, bone, spine ; paxos, a hedge ; plyita, to freeze ; pC(a, a root ; pvrov, rein ; pdovvvixt, to strengthen ; pGxrrat;, pillar, &c. Secondly, in several of the words mentioned by Plato the meaning of motion can easily be shown to be secondary, not primary. If K^pixari^iv, for instance, means to crumble, to cut into small slices, this is because Ktpixa means a small slice, and it does so because it is derived from ndpa, to shave, having been called originally a chip.

But I doubt whether a serious refutation of these remarks is justified. They are useful only as showing what latitude there is and must be in this subject. While modern speculators see an imitation of the blowing of the wind in the root VA, Plato sees or rather hears an imitation of what is windy in the sound of the letters <p, \j/, <r, and ( (1. c, pp. 427, 434), because their pronunciation is accompanied by great expenditure of breath. When Socrates considers LANGUAGE A.ND THOUGHT. 377 further that the closing and pressure of the tongue in the utterance of d and t was an expression of binding and rest, that 1 expressed smoothness, g clamminess, n inwardness, we must not forget that Hermogenes is made to reply immediately : ' 0 Socrates, Cratylos mystifies me ; he says that there is a fitness of names, but he never explains what fitness is.'

This is the right spirit in which such guesses should be treated. There may be some truth in them here and there, but even if there is, it is beyond our reach. Custom is so strong that we all imagine we perceive a certain appropriateness in a root STA meaning to stand or stop, in a root MAR meaning to rub, in a root TUD meaning to strike. There may be some truth in that fancy, but if we take a more comprehensive view of radical sounds and radical meanings, not only in the Aryan but likewise in the Semitic and Turanian languages, we soon find that our fancy is as often contradicted by the facts as it is confirmed by them. There seems to be neither necessity nor absolute freedom in the choice of the sounds expressive of our acts. Even those who imagine that they can detect some reasonableness in them, must confess that they have no means of testing or proving it. We can well understand that among the concomitant clamours of thought the struggle for existence must have been intense, though we have hardly any opportunities left for watching that struggle. If some scholars imagine they can know, or feel, why SAR expressed our consciousness of moving on, while VABH expressed our consciousness of weaving, we need not contradict them, but we could easily show that in other families of speech the same sounds have a totally different 378 LECTURE XIV.
meaning. Take, for instance, the root SAR in FinnoUgric. It means \ (1) to sprout forth, to bubble up, to rise; to be long, to be slim, to be straight ;
(2) to stir, to awake, to be busy ;
(3) to rub, to wipe, to whet, to shear ;
(4) to stir, to mix ; to make variegated, to grind, to defile ;
(5) to push away, to squeeze, to narrow, to break, to split, to wound; to suffer, to be oppressed, to shrink, to die ;
(6) to speak, to narrate.

Though Professor Donner, the highest authority on Finnish philology, treats all these meanings as modifications of one central concept, he would probably be willing to admit that possibly such meanings as to speak or to narrate might flow from an independent source, and have nothing in common with such concepts as sprouting, bubbling, stirring, and all the rest ; but other scholars might insist on babbling being but a modification of bubbling, and spouting of sprouting. Here, if anywhere in the study of language, much latitude must be allowed to personal dispositions and idiosyncrasies. We may be able to form a general idea how what we call roots survived in a conflict of ever so many possibilities, but we shall never be able to discover anything like necessity in the character of those historical roots which have been discovered by an analysis of real languages or families of language.
The conceptual foundation of Language .
But what is no longer a theory or a mere dream, 1 Donner, Vergleich. Wbrterb. cler Finnisch-Ugrischeu Sprachen, ii. p. 1.

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 379 but a simple fact, is that all our words are derived from these conceptual roots, and that all or nearly all of them signify originally some primitive acts of man. These are facts, and the only thing we have to supply is an explanation why language should have started from such roots, and not from the imitation of the sounds of nature. I do not go so far as other scholars in denying the possibility of any words being formed from mimetic sounds. After all, cuckoo is a word, though perhaps not in the strictest sense. To hush is a word which has come to express a concept. The same process which yielded such words might have been carried much further, so far as we know. But the fact remains that it was not, and what we have to explain is not what language might have been, but what it is. That roots expressing acts should have been the true feeders of language becomes intelligible when we consider that the earliest possible, or, I should say, the earliest inevitable concepts could not well have arisen under more natural and favourable circumstances than from our consciousness of our own repeated acts. Even man's bodily organism, his possessing two arms, two legs, two eyes, two ears, would have helped towards making him comprehend two as one; and the Dyad is the beginning of all that we call conceptual thought. Then would follow the consciousness of our own repeated continuous acts, and if such acts, particularly when performed in common, were accompanied by natural sounds, by sounds understood therefore by many people, the battle was won. Man knew what it was to have concepts and intelligible signs of concepts at the same time. Everything else, as we know from the 380 LECTUKE XIV.
history of language, would then follow as a matter of course.

We can see all this historical growth of language from its very beginning, most clearly in the Aryan languages, because they have been analysed most carefully. But the Semitic languages also tell us the same tale, only that here the formation of triliteral roots prevents us often from watching the earliest phases in the growth of roots and radical concepts. Triliteral roots must have been originally . biliteral, or monosyllabic, but though this can be proved in some cases, it cannot yet be done with perfect certainty in all. Here we must wait for new light from the most ancient Babylonian Inscriptions.

With the Finno-Ugric languages great progress has been made of late. Professor Donner, in his Comparative Dictionary of the Finno-Ugric Languages, is doing for that branch of human speech what others have done for the analysis of the Aryan and Semitic languages. The number of roots seems smaller here than in Sanskrit or Hebrew, and the growth and ramification of subsequent meanings become therefore all the more instructive.

Other families or classes of language have as yet been analysed with partial success only, still wherever a beginning has been made, the result has always been the same, and we may take it as a fundamental principle, admitted by all students of lauguage in whatever part of the world, that in the beginning there were roots and radical concepts, and that with these roots human speech was built up from beginning to end.

There are languages, like the ancient Chinese, in LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 381 which words and roots are identical, at least in outward appearance, where all is material, and nothing, as yet, purely formal. In such languages, whatever their age may be, we have again a tangible proof of the theory which we formed for ourselves, from an analysis of more developed languages, such as Sanskrit and Hebrew, namely that language begins with roots and thought with concepts, and that the two are one.
Our conceptual world.

When the human mind had once reached the conceptual stage, the battle was won, an entrance into the ideal world had been effected. With the first real word, a new world was created, the world of thought, our real home.

When we have once seen that thought, in its true sense, is always conceptual, and that every word is derived from a conceptual root, we shall no longer be surprised when we are told that words, being conceptual, can never stand for a single percept. There can be nothing in the world of sense corresponding even to such simple words as dog, tree, apple, table, to say nothing of colour, virtue, goodness and all the rest, for they are all conceptual. We can never expect to see a dog, a tree, an apple, or a chair. Dog means every kind of dog from the greyhound to the spaniel ; tree every kind of tree from the oak to the cherry ; apple every kind of apple from the pineapple to the pippin ; chair every kind of chair from the royal throne to the professorial chair. People often imagine that they can form a general image of a dog, by leaving out what is peculiar to every individual dog, or to every kind of dog. Let them try 382 LECTURE XIV.

the experiment, which Mr. Galton has tried for human faces, namely, photograph a greyhound, and over it a spaniel, and then a St. Bernard, and then a Scotch terrier, and so on till every breed has been superadded. They will then see what kind of general image they would arrive at, and they will strongly object to harbouring such monsters in their mind.

Here also Berkeley acted as a most resolute pioneer. He showed that it is simply impossible for any human being to make to himself a general image of a triangle, for such an image would have to be at the same time right-angled, obtuse-angled, acute-angled, equilateral, isosceles, and scalene. This is impossible,, whereas it is perfectly possible to have an image of any single triangle, to name some characteristic feature common to all triangles, namely their possessing three angles, and thus to form a name and at the same time a concept of a triangle. This mental process which Berkeley described so well as applied to modern concepts, we can watch with regard to all, even the most primitive concepts, if we examine the annals of language. Man discovered in a smaller or larger number of trees, before they were as yet trees to him, something which was interesting to him and which they all shared in common. Now trees were interesting to primitive man for various reasons, and they could have been named for every one of these reasons. For practical purposes, however, trees were particularly interesting to the primitive framers of language, because they could be split in two, cut, shaped into blocks and planks, shafts and boats. Hence from a root dar, to tear, they called trees dru or daru, lit. what can be split or torn or cut to pieces.

LANGUAGE AND THOUGHT. 383 From the same root they also called the skin bepp.a, because it was torn off, and a sack bopos, because it was made of leather (Sanskrit dWti), and a spear, bopv, because it was a tree, cut and shaped and planed.

Such words being once given, they would produce ever so many offshoots. The Gauls called their priests Druides \ the Irish drui, literally the men of the oak-groves. The Greeks called the spirits of the forest- trees Dryades ; and the Hindus called a man of wood, or a man with a wooden, or, as we say, flinty heart, daru?ia, cruel.

What applies to this single word for tree, applies to all words. They are all derived from roots, they are all conceptual, they all express something common to many things, and therefore something that can be thought of and spoken of, but can never be perceived with our senses as a single and real object.

If then we think in words, and in words only, is there anything in the world, I will not say now, more wonderful simply, but more momentous, more serious, more paramount for all our intellectual work than our words ? And if that is so, need we wonder that religion also has its deepest roots in language, nay would be perfectly inconceivable without language. It has often been said that numina are nominee, and if our line of argument hitherto has been straight, we shall not only accept this statement, but understand its true meaning. Try to realise Zeus or Hera without their names, and you will see that there is nothing to realise. But do not let us say therefore that Zeus and Hera are mere names. This expression, mere names, is one of the most objectionable and self-con- 1 Hibbert Lectures, John Rhys, p. 221.

tradictory expressions in the whole dictionary of philosophy. There is no such thing as a mere name, as little as there is a mere concept. There is something that was meant by Zeus and even by Hera, and though these names were weak, and tentative only, and exposed to all the dangers of mythology, yet the best among the Greeks never forgot what the name of Zeus was really intended for — the Infinite, it may be, the nameless Power behind all names. You all remember the words of Aeschylus in the Chorus of the Agamemnon — for who that has read them can ever forget them again : —

' Zeus, whoever he is, if this be the name by which he loves to be called — by this name I address him. For if I verily want to cast off the idle burden of my thought, proving all things, I cannot find any on whom to cast it, except Zeus alone V Aeschylus knew or divined what we want to prove, that religion is the language or interpretation of the Infinite. There may be nothing corresponding to Zeus, as pictured by Phidias, and as believed in by the people of Greece. But Zeus was not a mere name, for all that. It was but one out of many names by which the Greeks, and, as we shall see, not the Greeks only, but all the Aryas, tried to grasp the Infinite behind the Finite, tried to name the Unknown by the Known, tried to see the Divine behind the veil of nature.
1 Lectures on the Science of Language, ii. 485.
zh: 「沒有語言我們應該怎麼辦？」
在我們完成了對目前在文明世界各地所使用的語言，以及在我們所知人類在這個星球上出現的整個歷史時期中曾經使用過的語言的全面考察之後，現在應該提出這樣的問題：語言究竟是什麼？
現在我要問各位，你們知道世界上有什麼比語言更奇妙的東西嗎？¹ 誠然，即使我們無法說話，我們仍然能夠看見、聽聞、品嚐、嗅聞和感受。

我們能夠嚐到甜味而喜歡它，也能嚐到苦味而厭惡它。我們可能會因為火會燒傷而遠離它，轉身朝向水——因為水很涼爽，或因為水能解渴；但我們應該沒有詞彙來區分火和水，或熱和冷，或甜和苦。我們應該像那些燒傷手指而哭泣的孩童，像那些嚐過糖而微笑的孩童，像那些吞下醋而號啕大哭的孩童。有些人可能會稱這種逃離傷害之物、轉向令人愉悅之物的行為是理性的，正如他們說狗是理性的——因為當主人舉起木棒時，狗會逃離主人；當主人拿出一塊肉時，狗就會跳到他身上。
如果用一個大膽的隱喻把它稱為理性，我們無須反對，只要我們區分有意識與無意識、有言說與無言說的理性，並且記得，藉由以這種極度擴張的意義來使用理性，我們終將被迫連把眼睛閉上以躲避即將來臨的打擊，也稱為一種推理行為。
然而，無論有無語言，我們確實可以做到所有這些事情，甚至更多。我們可以戰鬥和殺戮，可以愛護和保護。如果我們極其聰慧，我們可以積累那些通過反覆遺傳而使我們的後代能夠築巢、挖掘地洞或建造蜂窩狀小屋的性情和習慣。最強壯的個體或許可以學會擔任哨兵並使自己受人服從；較弱的性別甚至可能發明危險信號和其他溝通的跡象。
我毫不懷疑，騎士精神的與非騎士精神的感受也可能在我們心中喚起，如同我們在高等動物身上所見者一般，而嫉妒與復仇心，以及友誼與愛慕，同樣可能影響我們的行為。
但即便如此，想像我們坐在這裡，互相凝視著彼此，帶著某種和善的牛般呆滯目光，卻沒有說出半句話語，不僅嘴上沒有，心中也沒有；我們的心智實際上只是一塊消極的感光板，我們無法抓住在其上描繪的任何輪廓，藉由說「這是這個」和「那是那個」而將其掌握住！
思維的定義1

有些哲學家，如你所知，認為人類與動物相同，儘管他們沒有語言，也仍能沉默地坐著思考。遺憾的是，他們沒有告訴我們他們對思考的含義是什麼，以及不是什麼，但似乎很清楚，他們把思考用作心智活動每一種形式的同義詞。笛卡兒（Des Cartes）在討論其基本原理「我思故我在」（Cogito ergo sum）時也是如此；但作為一位誠實的哲學家，他警告我們，他使用 cogitare 時採用最廣泛的意義，以便包含感覺、知覺、記憶、想像以及其他一切。如果思考（to think）的含義已被承認地擴張到這樣程度，那麼沒有人會夢想否認動物儘管無言，仍然可以思考，而且我們也可以不使用語言而思考，也就是說，不曾擁有過語言，不知道一個詞與另一個詞的區別。
我們在想什麼？

但現在讓我們問那些哲學家們一個簡單的問題：如果我們能夠不借助語言而進行思考，那麼我們在想什麼呢？究竟在想什麼呢？我並非想像法庭上的律師一樣設下陷阱。當然，如果你告訴我你在想什麼，你也只能用語言來表達。我也不聲稱自己能讀心術，在你沒有告訴我的情況下就能說出你在想什麼—因為我當然也只能用語言來做這件事。但我請你問問自己，如果你在思考什麼的話，你在想什麼；我也將參與這個實驗。假設我們都在思考著，正如我們所說的，一隻狗，那麼一旦我們試圖回答自己「我們在想什麼」的問題，qu'une chose qui pense?（「是一個懷疑的東西，聽見、認識、肯定、否定、願意或不願意、也想像、以及感受的東西」）
A a 354

我們只能藉著對自己或他人說「狗」（Dog）來做到這一點。誠然，canis、chien、Hund 也同樣可行，我們甚至不必在記起某隻狗或聽到周圍幾隻狗吠叫時發出這些詞的音聲。然而，雖然我們可以壓抑或遺忘曾經聽過的某個詞的音聲，甚至可以用另一種語言的詞彙來取代它，但我們絕不可能在沒有備有該詞的情況下，對自己的思想內容有所自覺——正如義大利人所說的，該詞必須「暗中保留著」（in petto），或如某些野蠻民族所說的，「藏在胃裡」。
用德文或英文思考。
如果對於沒有語言就不可能進行真實思維這一點，你心中仍有任何疑慮，那麼請問問自己：當你詢問一位長期居住在英國的外國人，他是用德語思考還是用英語思考時，你的意思究竟是什麼？倘若他回答說兩者都不是，你會說什麼？我相信你會認為，而且這個認為是正確的，那他就是個傻瓜。
我們為什麼無法不用言詞而思考。

但如果事實確實如此，如果思想在該詞嚴格限定的意義上是不可能沒有語言而產生的，那麼你可能會問，為什麼會這樣呢。許多人假設我們首先形成我們的思想，或如他們所說的我們的觀念，之後我們才去尋找某些聲音，將它們附加於我們的觀念之上，並且我們保留這些聲音是因為我們發現它們對於交流的目的非常有用。現在我問你，這樣的過程可能或可以想像嗎？我們是否曾發現自己擁有一個概念，但卻沒有其名稱，除非我們確實已經遺忘，並且知道我們已經遺忘了我們從前所擁有的那個名稱？或者在整個世界的任何地方，我們是否可以找到空洞的聲音，如父親和母親，尚未表達任何意義，但在需要時準備就用？我知道有些人談論不可名狀的思想，但他們指的是感受；另一些人說他們可能有某株植物的清晰概念，卻不知道它的名稱；他們談論那株植物，噢，你叫它什麼？但植物不是一個名稱嗎？蔬菜不是一個名稱嗎？物體不是一個名稱嗎？它不是一個名稱嗎？甚至「你叫它什麼」不是一個名稱嗎？

我們時常不知道確切或恰當的名稱，但在這種情況下，我們總是知道更為普遍的名稱。倘若我們從未見過或聽聞過象，我們不會知道它的名稱，但我們會知道它是一種動物，並如此稱呼它；我們會知道它是四足動物，並如此稱呼它。若我們不知道所見的究竟是無生命的塊狀物，抑或是植物、鳥類、魚類或哺乳動物，我們除了「物」這樣的名稱外，別無他名可呼。我們無法進一步為其命名，因為我們對之知之甚少，因為我們無法將其納入任何更確定的概念性名稱之下。我們也許能看到、聽聞和觸摸象，我們或許對它有多少有些精確的心像，但除非我們能夠述說或命名它的某種區別性特徵，否則我們既無法真正命名它，也無法在該詞的真正意義上認識它。
如同大多數哲學家所假設的，認為我們首先掌握著一支赤裸概念的軍隊，之後才給它們穿上語言的制服，這在兩個最簡單的理由之下是不可能的；首先，因為根本沒有倉庫可以
首先，因為只有語言才能提供這些言語的制服，其次，因為我們從未遇見過赤裸的概念；或者，更直言不諱地說，因為我們從未遇見過沒有皮毛的兔子，或沒有殼的牡蠣。

真正的思想之所以離不開語言，理由非常簡單。我們所謂的語言，並非一般人所想像的那樣，是「思想加上聲音」；相反地，我們所謂的思想，其實是「語言減去聲音」。也就是說，一旦我們掌握了語言，我們可以低聲哼出詞語，或在完全靜默中記住它們，就像我們記住一段樂曲時，聲帶完全沒有震動一樣。我們也可以縮減詞語，使「如果柏拉圖是對的」這類表達，足以代表一整座圖書館。事實上，我們甚至可以消除詞語的意義，使詞語僅作為符號留存¹；我們甚至可以用代數符號取代真正的詞語，從而進行計算或推理的過程，其最終結果令人驚嘆不已。然而，正如我們無法不使用實際或變形的數字來計算，我們也無法不使用實際或變形的詞語來推理。這就是語言科學引領我們達到的最終結論，而這個結論已將語言科學轉變為思想科學。「我們以詞語思考」——這必須成為未來一切精確哲學的憲章，我相信，它同時也將成為過去所有哲學體系的調和基礎。

> 「宗教學（the science of religion）的誕生，乃是人類思想史上最偉大的轉折之一。當我們發現，那些看似截然不同的宗教體系，其實不過是同一偉大真理的不同表達時，一種全新的理解便油然而生。」 我們必須謹記，宗教學（the science of religion）的首要任務，並非僅僅是收集與分類各種宗教現象，而是去理解這些現象背後所蘊含的共通本質。正如語言科學（the science of language）教導我們，所有語言皆源自人類心靈的同一種能力——即溝通的能力——宗教學也必須認識到，所有宗教皆源自人類對無限者（the Infinite）的感知與回應。這種感知，這種對超越自身之物的渴望，並非語言的產物，而是溝通本身——一種更深層、更原初的連結。因此，我們不應將宗教視為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），而應視為人類心靈與無限者之間永恆對話的見證。
但人們肯定會說，人類無需語言也能溝通，動物亦然。是的，*思維科學*，第35頁。
他們確實如此，我們也都如此，只是有些人較成功，有些人較不成功。波利尼西亞人，正如夏米索（Chamisso）在其迷人的《環球航行記》（1815-1818）中所告訴我們的，用字節省，一個眨眼往往取代長篇大論。即使在較不野蠻的種族中，或許也是如此。他們能避免時甚至不說「是」，只動動眉毛。只有對陌生人，他們才會說 *Inga*（是）。但若我們正確使用詞語，這種溝通並非思想。

我甚至更進一步，主張我們天生如此——無論我們喜歡與否，都必須透過外在跡象來展現內心的活動。很少有人能完全壓抑情緒，以至於在憤怒或快樂時不讓他人察覺。我們臉紅、顫抖、皺眉、噘嘴、咧嘴、大笑、微笑，還有什麼比這些不由自主的跡象更能洩露心聲，有時甚至更具說服力呢？我毫不懷疑動物也透過類似跡象洩露情感，而這些跡象能被同類理解。你只需擾亂一個蟻丘，看看會發生什麼。許多螞蟻會沿著牠們慣常的路徑逃竄，攔住每一隻遇到的螞蟻，而每隻螞蟻在經過觸碰與交流後，會以同樣的急切跑回蟻丘協助救援，就像消防隊員在街上聽到號角後奔向火場一樣。我們無法理解這是如何做到的，但那顆不比針頭大的螞蟻小腦袋，必定能夠表達恐懼並懇求幫助，正如狗會跑向主人一樣¹。 ¹ 沙米索作品集，第一卷，第357頁。
你會在牠的臉上看到恐懼，並透過牠的動作懇求你的幫助。但人們何時才會明白，情緒並非思想，而如果我們將憤怒或喜悅稱為思想，那隻不過是混淆了我們自己的思緒，並擾亂了我們自己的語言？

我相信，我們情感中某些不自覺的表現，隨著時間推移，可能會發展為有意識的手勢；我們從默劇，也從據說在美洲與澳洲、使用不同語言的部落之間進行的溝通中得知，這種手勢語言可以達到極高的完善程度。但我們不能忘記，在所有觀察到手勢溝通的情況中，相關各方各自都擁有一套真正的語言；事實上，他們首先是用自己的概念語言思考，然後再將思想轉譯回默劇之中。然而，這個主題相當奇特，值得比迄今為止更多的研究。我們自以為能理解，為何一個人跪下被認為是在懇求憐憫，為何另一個人揮舞拳頭被認為是在說：「退開！」但這些手勢在不同國度使用時，並非總有相同的含義；即便是富有表現力的¹，也是如此。 ¹ 在加那利群島中的戈梅拉島上，人們透過一種口哨語言進行溝通。該島被許多從中央高原向四面八方延伸的深邃峽谷與沖溝所貫穿。這些峽谷沒有橋樑，往往極難跨越，因此，那些直線距離上其實住得很近的人，若要會面，往往得繞上好幾個小時的路。於是，吹口哨便成了一種極佳的溝通方式，並逐漸發展成足以真正替代言語的規模。但他們吹口哨所表達的，仍是他們自己的語言。
聾啞人士所使用的符號，在全球各地絕非完全相同¹。
孩童在學會說話之前，早已能夠模仿進食、飲水、騎在父親膝上等動作，藉此表達他們微小的願望；但願望並非思想，正如恐懼與驚駭亦非思想。倘若某些哲學家樂意將這些情感狀態稱為思想，他們大可自行承擔風險，但無論如何都應事先告知，好讓他人能夠對這種恣意妄為有所防範。

然而，在我們能夠充分理解這些最古老文獻的價值之前，我們必須先對人類心智的原始狀態，以及語言與思想之間那種奇妙的互動關係，形成某種清晰的概念。在宗教學中，如同在語言科學中一樣，我們必須追溯到最古老的根源，並觀察那些最初的表徵如何逐漸形成。我們必須學習理解，那些對我們而言如今僅是抽象名稱的事物，最初是如何作為生動的意象而存在的；我們必須重新捕捉那些早已消逝的感受，正是這些感受賦予了這些意象以生命與力量。當我們閱讀《梨俱吠陀》中的讚歌時，我們所面對的並非精心構築的神學體系，而是自然力量的直接詩意表達。太陽並非僅僅是一個發光的天體；祂是一位神聖的存在，一位驅散黑暗的戰士，一位賜予生命與財富的君王。黎明並非一個抽象的時間概念；祂是一位容光煥發的少女，一位揭開面紗的新娘，一位驅逐黑夜的母親。這些意象並非刻意為之的修辭手法；它們是人類心智最初且最自然的表達方式。對原始人而言，世界充滿了生命；每一種力量，每一種運動，每一種變化，都是一個意志的行動，一個人格的存在。正是在這種萬物有靈的直覺中，我們發現了宗教最深的根源，以及神話最豐富的源頭。語言本身，在其最初的階段，並非一套約定俗成的符號，而是一系列活生生的隱喻；而當這些隱喻的原始意義被遺忘時，它們便凝固為神話，或者如我們有時所說，成為「語言的疾病」。因此，要理解宗教的起源，我們就必須學會解讀這些古老的意象，並追溯它們從自然現象到神聖人格的演變過程。

一些更為嚴肅的哲學家主張圖像的地位。他們說，感官留在我們記憶中的圖像，當然可以被稱為思想。這或許可行，但前提是我們讓他人知道，在我們自己的哲學用語中，我們是以那種廣義的方式使用「思考」一詞。然而，區分並保留「想像力」一詞來指稱圖像的運作，顯然更為妥當。我個人認為，人類若未先以名稱構築圖像，便不可能擁有真實的圖像；而在生理學家中，赫爾姆霍茲（Helmholtz）也否認我們能在沒有名稱的情況下擁有知覺。不過，當然，如果像高爾頓先生（Mr. Galton）這樣細心的觀察者向我們保證，他們擁有圖像卻不知道這些圖像是什麼，也不記得它們的名稱，那麼我們理應相信他們，即使我們無法理解他們。他們顯然急於證明：動物雖然沒有語言，卻擁有圖像；牠們能組合這些圖像；而牠們的行為、牠們的感官活動——或者如他們喜歡稱呼的¹——都以此為基礎。 ¹ 馬勒裡（Mallery），《北美印第安人的手語》（Sign Language among the North-American Indians）。

牠們，即牠們的理性行為，是由這些所決定的。姑且如此假定，至少為了論證的緣故。但即便如此，這種沒有語言的想像，甚至這種沒有語言的推理，難道不與有語言的想像和推理截然不同嗎？假設有一條狗，不像我養的某條狗那樣——牠先向我表達不安，然後把我拖到牠那張紅色的毯子上，向我展示那張毯子被我另一條本該躺在自己藍色毯子上的狗佔據了，接著用責備的眼神看著我，直到我命令那條狗離開，最後以受冤無辜者的全部驕傲佔領自己的紅毯——假設這條狗，不是用哀求和吠叫，而是突然用後腿站起來對我說：「那條狗佔了我的毯子；先生，請您命令牠離開。」我們豈不是會幾乎嚇瘋？或者，讓我們把一個嬰兒和一個成年人並排擺放：一個掙扎哭喊著要一杯牛奶，另一個則清楚地說：「我想要那杯牛奶。」這兩種行為之間的距離難道不是無法估量的嗎？前者僅僅是我們感官直接或反射作用的結果，後者則是歷經數千年發展的成果？成年人若渴得要死，無疑也可能衝向杯子，一言不發地喝下，我們或許會將他急切表情的流露稱為語言，將他衝過去的動作稱為理性。但使用這種隱喻式的語言，我們一無所獲。有些哲學家告訴我們，一個嬰兒伸出雙臂，不可能不經歷一個無聲的三段論：「透過伸出雙臂，我們得到我們想要的東西；我想要這杯牛奶，因此我伸出雙臂。」或許如此，但我們對此一無所知，也永遠不會知道，直到嬰兒能夠說話——也就是說，直到他不再是嬰兒——然後告訴我們他在想什麼。

然而，從嬰兒到能夠說話的成人，其間相隔的不只是十或二十年的光陰。他所接受的語言，是數千年來智力勞動的成果。我們詞語與思想的原始構築，是一個除了語言地質學家之外，無人能稍有概念的程序；而認為一個人能在十年或二十年內累積其文法與詞彙的豐富內涵，就如同相信地球及其山河能在六日內被創造出來一樣。令人費解的是，這個早已被反覆駁斥、令人厭煩的論點，竟一再地被提出。嬰兒與鸚鵡在一段時間內確實都沒有語言，而兩者後來也都學會說「你好嗎？」——這點千真萬確。然而，孩子學會的是人類的語言，鸚鵡卻從未說過鸚鵡語。
Involuntary and voluntary sounds.
繼所謂手勢語之後的下一步，引領我們進入非自願與自願的聲音。我將非自願的聲音稱為感嘆詞，它們具有直接的自然起源，用以表達喜悅、恐懼、憤怒、讚嘆、贊同或反對。對於習慣自身感嘆詞的我們而言，這些聲音似乎具有自然的適切性；但在此處，比較研究同樣告訴我們，事實並非如此。

所以，舉例而言，*no* 並不總是表示「不」；在敘利亞語中它表示「是」。即使在愛爾蘭語中，我們也發現表示「不」的詞不僅有 *naicc*，還有 *aicc*¹。我稱之為「自發感嘆詞」的，是諸如以 *how wow* 指狗、以 *moo* 指牛這類模仿聲音的詞。然而在此我們也發現，那些我們認為完全自然且可理解的詞，並非總是如此。對我們的耳朵而言，狗叫聲是 *bow wow*，但對德國人的耳朵來說，牠叫的是 *Miff klaff*。
以我們的字母表來記錄不成音節的聲音極為困難。許多人曾嘗試寫下鳥類發出的聲響，但迄今為止成果甚微。一位精通生理語音學最新理論的偉大語音學家，耗費了許多日夜觀察夜鶯的鳴囀；各位認為他的轉寫結果如何？夜鶯真正的鳴聲，經化約為字母書寫後，便是：
抱歉，您提供的「英文原文」看起來並非英文，而是類似拼音或轉寫的文字，無法進行翻譯。請提供正確的英文原文，我將依照您的要求進行繁體中文翻譯。

您知道，在語言被科學研究之前，人們普遍認為所有人類言語都是這樣產生的，而我們詞彙的最終元素是對自然聲音的模仿或不由自主的感嘆。我稱這些理論為「汪汪理論」與「呸呸理論」。近來有些哲學家又增添了第三種理論，稱之為「呦嗬嗬理論」，但這其實只是「呸呸理論」的一個分支。由於一個極其離譜的誤解，這個理論被歸給了諾瓦雷（Noire），而他其實是該理論最堅定的反對者之一。根據這個理論，語言直接源自¹ Zeuss, *Grammatica Celtica*。古愛爾蘭語中的「是」為 *iss ed*，即「est hoc」，或簡寫為 *ed*，=€k>th. ita。——惠特利‧斯托克斯（Whitley Stokes）。
人們在從事拉、磨、挖、划船及類似原始活動時所發出的呼喊聲。
在此，支持「喲－嘿－呵」理論的人，無疑觸及了語言與思想發展中一個非常重要的階段，我們稍後便會明白；然而，若他們將「喲－嘿－呵」這類聲音僅視為感嘆詞，那麼他們仍深陷於「呸－呸」理論的苦境中，也就是說，他們甚至尚未察覺到自己所欲解決的問題之困難所在。
汪汪理論、噗噗理論與吆喝理論這些名稱，有時被批評為過於粗俗，甚至可能帶有冒犯意味。然而，由於這些理論的原始形式已無任何學者採納，這些名稱實際上毫無殺傷力，且確實有其用處——因為它們不言自明。倘若我們畏避這些名稱，就必須使用「模仿論」、「擬聲論」或「感嘆論」這類冗長術語，而每個術語都需要詳盡的註解來說明。
汪汪聲、呸呸聲與勞動呼號理論
然而，這三種理論在當時絕非如我們今日所見那般不合邏輯。它們無疑是先驗（a priori）理論，但確實有某些事實作為支撐。每一種語言中都有感嘆詞，而根據語言的一般類比，其中一些感嘆詞已發展為動詞、形容詞與名詞。例如「Hush」（德語的 *husch*）便是一個感嘆詞：在德語中，它用來驅趕鳥類、表達任何快速動作，或吸引注意；而在英語中，它如今主要用於……
> 用來命令保持沉默。從這個感嘆詞（而非任何詞根）衍生出德文的形容詞 *husch*（意為「快」）與名詞 *Husch*（意為「迅速」，亦指一擊、一記耳光）。因此，莎士比亞《哈姆雷特》中的詩句： > 我們不過是魯莽地 > 在偷偷摸摸中將他埋葬。
「在德文中被譯為：Und thoricht war's von uns, so unter'm Husch Ihn zu bestatten.」
此外，德文中有一個陰性名詞 *Die Husche*，意指一陣驟雨，以及兩個動詞：*huschen*（快速移動）與 *husch eln*（草率完成工作）。在英語中，*to hush* 則專指「命令安靜」或「使平靜」之意。
這便是「呸呸理論」（Pooh-pooh theory）的一個例證。
**汪汪理論**（Boiv-woiv theory）可援引若干詞彙為證，其中最著名者為「杜鵑」：希臘文 *κόκκυξ*（kokkyx）、拉丁文 *cuculus*、梵文 *kokila*。希臘文中尚有動詞 *κοκκύζειν*（kokkyzein），其重疊完成式為 *κέκοκκυκα*（kekokkyka），意為「發出杜鵑啼叫」。
「喲－嘿呵」理論（Yo-heho theory）實為「呸－呸」理論（Pooh-pooh theory）的一個分支，但可藉由「砰！」（bang！）——伴隨打擊動作的感嘆詞——來說明：to bang（猛擊）、to beat violently（痛毆），以及近來備受讚賞的banged hair（蓬亂髮型）。
收集所有無論是英語、德語、拉丁語、希臘語或梵語中，直接由感嘆詞元素構成的詞語，會是一個極其有趣的課題。而這比任何事物都更能教導我們：在我們為人類語言這片無定形的底層聲索了一切理應歸屬於它的部分之後，我們其實僅僅攻佔了外圍的工事，而語言的真正堡壘仍未觸及。

在我們能夠充分理解宗教學的目標與方法之前，有必要先追溯其根源。這門科學並非憑空而生，而是人類思想長期發展的產物，特別是與語言科學（the science of language）的進步密不可分。正如比較神話學（comparative mythology）揭示了神話思維的深層結構，宗教學也試圖透過比較不同宗教傳統，來探尋宗教現象的本質與演變規律。我們必須認識到，對「無限者」（the Infinite）的追尋，無論是在雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）還是圖蘭語族（Turanian）的傳統中，都展現出驚人的相似性與差異性。例如，在吠陀（Veda）文獻中，我們看到從多神教（polytheism）到單一神教（henotheism）乃至輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的過渡，而《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中的特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）等概念，則為我們提供了理解早期神祇觀唸的關鍵線索。穆勒（Müller）曾指出，神話的起源往往源於「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），這一洞見同樣適用於宗教觀唸的形成過程。因此，宗教學不僅是一門歷史學科，更是一門比較科學，它要求我們以嚴謹的態度，去分析那些塑造了人類精神世界的根本力量。

那座堡壘無法以強攻奪取，只能透過正規圍城；——它不會向先驗論證投降，只會屈服於後驗分析。這項分析由比較語言學的奠基者——博普、格林、波特等人——所執行；然而早在兩千多年前，梵文文法學家便已嘗試過。他們選取梵文——雅利安語言中最豐富且最原始的語言之一——透過對每個詞彙進行仔細分析，亦即分離所有可被分離且證明僅屬形式的成分，成功發現了某些無法再進一步分析的要素，並因此將其視為語言的最終事實，稱之為「詞根」。
然而，這些古代梵文文法學家所承認的詞根數量實在過於龐大。我們現已將其數量縮減至約八百個——我相信未來還會進一步減少——並藉此解釋梵文文獻中所有真正重要的詞彙。在較近代的語言中，許多源自同一詞根的詞群已然消亡，其位置被其他詞根的二次或三次派生詞所取代；因此，據稱收錄二十五萬詞彙的《牛津英語詞典》（現正於牛津出版），其詞根數量不超過約四百六十個。
1 Science of Thought，第 210 頁。

若要解釋莎士比亞、米爾頓與拜倫所說過的一切，這些詞根是必需的。但不止於此：這八百個梵文詞根所傳達的獨立概念，其數量並非八百，甚至遠低於此，而是已縮減至一百二十一這個小數目。憑藉這一百二十一個基本概念，人類大腦中曾有過的每一個思想，都能夠、也確實被表達出來。這在柏拉圖與亞里斯多德聽來，簡直如同痴人說夢，甚至對洛克與康德而言亦然；然而，這是一個無可置疑的事實，正如整個大自然的萬花筒——我們這個千變萬化的世界中所曾見過的一切——皆由大約六十種基本元素構成，同樣無可置疑。

我們發現，這些詞根大多表達動作，例如：擊打、挖掘、摩擦、碾碎、搗碎、切割、收集、混合、灑水、焚燒——這些動作實際上代表了人類某些原始的活動，但透過概括、專門化與隱喻，它們已被用來表達我們這個先進社會中最抽象的概念。一個意為「擊打」的詞根，為「一筆好生意」和「一針見血的評論」提供了名稱。「挖掘」一詞後來衍生出「搜尋」與「探究」之意。「摩擦」被用來表示「磨損」、「軟化」、「安撫」；「焚燒」則演變為「熱愛」，也指「羞愧」；而「收集」一詞，在原始邏輯中出色地承擔了我們現在所謂「觀察事實」、「大前提與小前提的聯繫」，甚至「三段論」的表達功能。
現在我們必須收束自己論證的線索。
我們已看到，沒有詞語，真正的思想是不可能的。我們現在又看到，所有詞語都由詞根構成，而這些詞根最初表達的是原始的合作行為，亦即人類在社會生活最早階段所會從事的那些行為。
從概念詞根派生而來的詞語。

但這還不是全部。讓我們記住，最初動搖那些自認能將語言中所有詞彙解釋為自然聲音模仿者之信念的，是一個奇異的事實：在動物的名稱中，完全找不到這類聲音的痕跡。在杜鵑鳥被當作典型案例、被視為「汪汪理論」的重大戰利品或圖騰之後，其他一切便都崩潰了。狗的名稱中沒有汪汪聲的痕跡，馬的名稱中沒有嘶鳴聲的痕跡，驢的名稱中沒有叫聲的痕跡，牛的名稱中沒有哞哞聲的痕跡。相反地，人們發現，每一個從詞根派生而來的詞語，都表達了一個普遍概念。馬的名稱——拉丁文 *equus*、梵文 *asva*——源自一個意為「尖銳或快速」的詞根；由此可見，馬是被構想並命名為「奔跑者」或「賽跑者」。同一個詞根還衍生出石頭、矛、針、尖端、視覺敏銳、思維敏捷等詞語，甚至直到新大陸的「機靈」（'cuteness）。
蛇的名稱來自一個意為「爬行」的詞根，而牠的另一個名稱——梵文 *ahi*、希臘文 *e^u*——則源自一個意為「扼殺」的詞根。
太陽（Sun，哥德語 siinna）源自詞根 *su*，意為「產生」；兒子（son，哥德語 sunns）則出自同一詞根。
> 詞根，以被動意義而言，原意為「所生者」、子嗣（filius）。
手（hand）源自一個在哥德語中作 *hinthan*（意為「抓取」）的詞根。
眼睛（Eye）、哥德語的 augo、拉丁文的 oc-ulus、梵文的 aksha，全都源自同一個詞根，其原意為「指向、刺穿」。在梵文中，眼睛的另一個名稱是 netra，意為「引導者」，來自詞根 ni（引導）。
所以，我們可以無止盡地追溯每個詞彙的根源及其原始概念。我並非意指我們在每個案例中都能成功。仍有許多詞彙尚未被揭露其隱秘的歷史，而批判性詞源學家仍有大量工作有待完成。

有許多詞彙完全無需梵文知識即可進行詞源解釋，我們經常使用它們卻從未思考其詞源意義。例如，一張長凳（settle）顯然是我們坐的東西；同樣地，一具馬鞍（saddle）雖較間接，也是坐具；一條道路（road）是我們騎乘之處；一個站立處（stand）是我們站立之地；一具棺架（bier）是承載我們的東西；一個負擔（burden）是我們自己承擔之物；一根箭桿（shaft）是被刨削或刨平之物；一份草稿（draft）是被拖曳之物；一股漂流物（drift）是被驅動之物；一條裂縫（rift）是被撕裂之物。一陣喜悅的顫慄（thrill），或一個令人毛骨悚然的故事，兩者皆源自「刺穿」（to thrill）、「穿透」（to pierce）、「穿孔」（to perforate）；但「無聊的話」（to bore）一詞，無論其歷史起源為何，如今則用來表達那種緩慢、迂迴、令人煩躁的談話，往往使人咬牙切齒¹。那麼，你可以將以下視為既定事實：除了少數擬聲詞的殘留之外，我們的詞彙基本上都是概念性的；它們源自概念性的詞根，或者換句話說，我們的詞彙就是概念。因此，如果承認我們只能透過概念來思考，那麼我們為何只能透過詞彙來思考，也就容易理解了。 ¹ 關於「bore」一詞的引入，參見《學院》雜誌，1月5日、12日，《語言與思想》，第369頁。
沒有語詞，概念可能存在嗎？
但您或許會問：難道沒有詞語，概念就無法存在嗎？當然不能，不過為了應對所有可能的異議，我們可以說：沒有符號——無論是詞語或其他任何東西——就沒有概念。若有人追問，究竟是概念先存在，而符號隨後才出現？我的回答是：不；兩者是同時發生的。但嚴格來說，符號作為概念的條件，實際上可說先於概念。經過一段時間後，詞語可能被遺忘，而正是在我們試圖回想那個催生概念的舊詞時，才會誤以為概念先於詞語存在。

> “宗教學（the science of religion）的奠基者，若我們能將此榮譽歸於一人，那便是伯克萊主教（Bishop Berkeley）。他率先明確區分了自然宗教（natural religion）與啟示宗教（revealed religion），並指出前者必須以比較神學（comparative theology）為基礎。然而，他的《阿爾西弗龍》（*Alciphron*）雖充滿洞見，卻未能開創一門新科學。宗教學的真正誕生，有待於語言科學（the science of language）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的興起，後者揭示了神話不過是「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），而多神教（polytheism）與單一神教（henotheism）或輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的種種形式，皆源於人類對無限者（the Infinite）的原始直覺。雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的諸神，從吠陀（Veda）的提婆（Deva）到希臘的特尤斯（Dyaus），無不印證此理。梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）與奧義書（Upanishad）中的讚歌，更為我們提供了從多神教過渡至一神教（monotheism）的關鍵線索。”

我從自身經驗深知，要清楚看清這一點有多麼困難。我們太習慣於不借助詞語來思考——也就是說，在拋棄詞語之後——以致幾乎無法認識到，最初若無這些或其他符號，任何概念性思維都是不可能的。若非有人告知，任何強壯的人都不會相信，最初若無學步帶，他根本無法行走。柏克萊（Berkeley）似乎終其一生都在與這個問題搏鬥；而他一向誠實，因此向我們呈現了他不同時期所達到的、最相互矛盾的結論。這是他哲學的基本原則之一。

概念，亦即當時所謂的一般觀念，若非將一個詞語或符號附著於知覺（即他所謂的特殊觀念）之上，便不可能形成。因此他明白，沒有詞語就沒有概念，沒有概念就沒有推論思維。然而儘管如此，他卻時常對這些詞語感到惱怒，並在其《人類理解原理論》（1710年）的緒論中寫道：「既然詞語如此容易誤導理解〔我決意在探究中盡可能少用它們〕：無論我思考何種觀念，我將努力讓它們赤裸裸地呈現在我眼前，盡我所能將那些因長期頻繁使用而與觀念緊密相連的名稱，排除在我的思想之外。」此外，在他的《備忘錄》（《著作集》，弗雷澤編，第一卷，第152頁）中，他又說：「若人們在思考時能擱置詞語，那麼除了事實問題之外，他們絕不可能犯錯。我的意思是，他們似乎不可能對某件實際上不真實的事情，確信不疑地斷言其為真。當然，在單純的知覺上我不會出錯。只要我們在推理中能不借助符號，我們便擁有確定的知識。誠然，在藉助符號進行的長篇推論中，可能會有記憶上的疏漏。」他如此這般對詞語——這些正是他本人所證明，若無之則概念不可能存在，或者至少與我們思想緊密相連的符號——發洩了不滿之後，又在另一處（第四卷，第455頁）迸發出以下言論：¹ ¹ 方括號內的愛爾蘭式矛盾語句，在第二版中被刪除。
語言與思想。371 讚頌：「語詞（他們藉此指涉各式各樣的符號）是如此必要，與其說它們（當適當運用或就其本身性質而言）有害於知識的進展或阻礙知識，毋寧說即便在數學本身中，若無它們也就不可能有任何論證。」

我認為，大多數現代哲學家在思想與語言的關係上，與柏克萊（Berkeley）同樣處於模糊不清的狀態；只是他們對自己沒有那麼誠實。例如，這位主教在其《隨筆錄》（Common-place Book）的另一段（第四卷，第429頁）中，在說服自己「用文字來記錄我們對自己的想法，或在某些私人沉思中使用文字，是荒謬的」之後，又插入以下註解打斷自己：「那麼，推論性的思想是否獨立於語言？」他忘了自己早已給出答案，亦即：它並非獨立，也不可能獨立。

我們現在必須追問：在人類心智從具體到抽象、從有限到無限的漫長旅程中，語言究竟扮演了什麼角色？這個問題將我們直接引向宗教學最核心的奧秘之一。因為，如果沒有語言，人類便無法固定其思想，也無法將那些稍縱即逝的感知與情感轉化為可以傳遞的概念。然而，語言本身卻是一把雙刃劍：它既是啟示之光，也是迷霧之源。正是這種雙重性，使得我們有必要仔細審視「命名的過程」（Process of naming）。 當原始人首次試圖為他所經驗到的力量——無論是雷鳴、風暴、日出，還是那孕育萬物的蒼穹——賦予一個名稱時，他實際上是在進行一種神聖的活動。他將一個具體的、有限的現象，與某種超越其直接呈現的、更為宏大的力量聯繫起來。例如，當雅利安人（Aryan）的先祖仰望蒼穹，並稱之為「特尤斯」（Dyaus）——這個詞在梵語中意為「照耀者」或「明亮之父」——時，他並非僅僅在描述一個物理空間。他是在將那無限的、支撐萬物的蒼穹，與父性的生育力量聯繫起來。這個名稱「特尤斯」後來演變為希臘的「宙斯」（Zeus）、羅馬的「朱庇特」（Jupiter），以及我們語言中的「星期二」（Tuesday）中的「Tyr」——這一切都指向同一個原初的直覺。 然而，正是在這個命名過程中，語言的陷阱開始顯現。一個名稱，一旦被創造出來，便獲得了某種獨立的存在。它原本是為了指向那無限者（the Infinite），但隨著時間的流逝，人們往往會忘記這個名稱的原始意涵，而將其與它所代表的具體現象或圖像混淆。這就是穆勒（Müller）所說的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。這種疾病導致了神話的誕生，也導致了從對單一至高者的敬畏，墮落為對多種自然力量的崇拜。原本指向「無限者」的單一神教（henotheism）或輪換主神教（kathenotheism）——即在一段時間內只崇拜一位至高神，但並不否認其他神祇的存在——便逐漸退化為多神教（polytheism）。 因此，要理解宗教的起源與演變，我們就必須成為語言的考古學家。我們必須挖掘那些被塵封的詞根，追溯那些神聖名稱的歷史變遷。在吠陀（Veda）的讚歌中，尤其是在《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）裡，我們可以看到這種命名的過程仍在進行之中。詩人們反覆吟詠著「提婆」（Deva）——意為「光明者」或「天神」——並將各種自然現象人格化。但他們從未完全忘記，所有這些名稱最終都指向一個統一的、不可言說的實在。而在後來的《奧義書》（Upanishad）中，這種對統一性的追求則變得更加明確，思想家們開始直接探討「梵」（Brahman）——那超越一切名稱與形式的終極實在。 比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的任務，正是要透過這些語言的層層沉積，去辨識出人類對無限者的原始直覺。這並非一項簡單的工作，因為語言科學（the science of language）告訴我們，詞義的變化是無常的。一個詞可能從崇高墮入庸俗，也可能從具體昇華為抽象。但正是透過這種嚴謹的比較研究，我們才能夠看清，在雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）以及圖蘭語族（Turanian）等不同語系的神話與宗教中，存在著驚人的結構相似性。這些相似性並非偶然，而是源自人類心智面對無限者時所共有的認知模式與命名衝動。

假設我們在雪、牛奶、粉筆與亞麻布上看見同一種顏色。除非我們有一個符號或工具來進行這項操作，否則我們無法將它單獨區分出來、從它所出現的不同感官對象中將其抽離或抽象化；而這個符號，就一般思維的日常用途而言，就是一個詞，例如「白」。在該詞出現之前，我們可能擁有不同的感覺，但嚴格意義上說，既沒有概念，甚至也沒有知覺。正是詞語的電火花，將各種感覺所共有的某種東西轉變為知覺；隨後，它又將各種知覺所共有的某種東西轉變為概念。
並將各種概念中的共通之處提昇至一個更高的概念。
弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒 但那股電光火石從何而來？人類從何處找到那個符號，用以指涉眾多事物？而那個符號，為了能夠適用於不同的感知，難道不早已需要某種具備概括性或概念性的特質嗎？

我們必須區分兩種起源：一是宗教的歷史起源，二是宗教的心理起源。前者涉及宗教在人類歷史中首次出現的時間與地點；後者則探討宗教觀念如何在人類心智中萌生。穆勒主張，宗教的根源在於人對「無限者」（the Infinite）的感知——這種感知並非來自外在啟示，而是源於感官經驗的內在超越。當人觀察自然現象（如日出、雷電、星辰運行）時，有限的事物會引發對無限的聯想；例如，從「可見的光」推想到「不可見的光源」。這種從有限到無限的跳躍，正是宗教意識的起點。 穆勒進一步指出，語言在這一過程中扮演關鍵角色。原始人為自然現象命名時，往往賦予它們人格化的特質；例如，希臘文的「宙斯」（Zeus）與梵文的「特尤斯」（Dyaus）皆源自同一詞根，意指「發光者」。然而，隨著語言演變，這些原本隱喻性的名稱逐漸被視為獨立實體，導致多神教（polytheism）的產生。穆勒因此稱神話為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）——一種因詞語原意被遺忘而產生的誤解。
是的，確實如此。而這正是整個語言哲學的**關鍵點（punctum saliens）**。早在人們追問人類如何獲得詞語之前，便已存在一個更古老的問題：人類如何獲得概念。幾乎所有哲學家都將人與獸的分界線劃在概念之上。在此之前，兩者似乎並無二致。
於是問題浮現：人類如何獨自超越知覺，進而抵達概念？

常見的回答是：人擁有某種特殊的天賦或官能，使他能夠形成概念，並將多樣者理解為一體。即便到了現在，仍有許多哲學家滿足於這種神話式的解釋。但這種回答根本不成其為回答。我們同樣可以說，人之所以開始書寫，是因為他擁有書寫的官能。我們想知道的是：究竟是什麼力量迫使——無論他願不願意——人去形成概念？為什麼他不滿足於感官所給予他的東西——看見這個、聽見那個？為什麼他必須超越單一意象，去追尋普遍者？他本可以在感覺、知覺與意象的世界裡過得十分快樂。為什麼他竟要離開那個世界？

我們所謂的語言根源，洩露了其中的奧秘。正如我們所見，幾乎所有根源都表達了人類的共同行為。如今，在人類尚未意識到任何客體作為客體之前，他無法不意識到自己的行為；而由於這些行為大多是重複且持續的，他無需新的努力，便能將自己的眾多或重複行為視為一個整體。此處正是最原始——我甚至可以說，第一個無可避免的概念——的起源：它們存在於我們對自身重複行為的意識中，將其視為一種連續的動作。例如，「摩擦」不僅僅是一次摩擦，然後再一次，然後又一次，而是持續的摩擦動作，後來引申為平滑、軟化、安撫；於是，原本意為「摩擦」的詞根，隨著時間演變為「安撫諸神的憤怒」。從我們說「哦，上帝，發發慈悲！」到我們最早的祖先說「諸神啊，被撫平吧，被磨順吧，被軟化吧！」之間，存在一條不間斷的鏈條或發展脈絡。

在我們開始探討宗教學的真正原則之前，有必要先回顧一下前人提出的各種理論。這些理論雖然未能完全解釋宗教現象的複雜性，但它們為我們提供了寶貴的經驗教訓。有些學者試圖將所有宗教歸結為一種原始直覺或本能，認為人類天生具有對神靈的感知；另一些人則主張宗教起源於對自然力量的敬畏，特別是對太陽、月亮、星辰等天體的崇拜。還有一種觀點認為，宗教是由狡猾的祭司或統治者為了控制民眾而發明的。然而，這些理論都未能充分說明宗教的多樣性及其在不同文化中的具體表現形式。正如語言科學的發展徹底改變了我們對人類語言的理解一樣，宗教學也需要一種更為嚴謹和比較性的方法。我們必須認識到，宗教並非單一現象，而是人類心靈對無限者（the Infinite）的多種回應方式。透過比較神話學（comparative mythology）和比較神學（comparative theology）的研究，我們可以追溯這些回應的歷史發展軌跡，從最原始的單一神教（henotheism）和輪換主神教（kathenotheism），到更為成熟的一神教（monotheism）或多神教（polytheism）。

現在或許可以明白，為何關於語言與思想起源的三種舊理論——「呸呸說」（Pooh-pooh theory）、「汪汪說」（Bow-wow theory）與「呦嗬說」（Yo-heho theory）——完全無法解釋真正需要解釋的問題，亦即概念詞語是如何產生的。「咕咕」可以是杜鵑啼聲的模仿，「汪汪」是狗吠聲的模仿，「呸呸」是我們輕蔑之情的模仿，「呦嗬」是我們勞動時的模仿；然而，憑藉這一切，我們永遠無法走出純粹感官認知的魔咒圈。我們需要的是概念性的聲音。我們該如何獲得它們？

此處，我日後將稱之為「協作理論」（Synergastic theory）的優勢，便會立刻顯現。若如我們所知，原始狀態的人們會以聲音伴隨其大多數日常行為，那麼這些行為的「伴隨喧囂」（clamor concomitans）便非單一行為的記號，而是我們對自身多次重複行為視為同一行動之意識的不可分割的伴隨物。在此，我們看見了概念思維的初曙。一旦清楚領悟此點，便同樣會明白：此概念語言起源理論與舊有的擬聲理論（onomatopoetic theories）之間的差異，並非程度之別，而是種類之異；其在語言科學（Science of Language）中所標誌的進步，遠比哥白尼理論（Copernican theory）在天文學（Science of Astronomy）中所帶來的更為重大。此處正是諾瓦雷（Noire）的真正功績所在。他是首位察覺到概念的自然起源存在於我們對自身行為之意識中的人。我得以透過指出梵語（Sanskrit）中幾乎所有詞根皆表達我們的行為，來為此提供證明。那些看不見我們追問概念詞根起源時所必須解釋的困難之人，或許會認為舊有的「呸呸理論」（Pooh-pooh）與「汪汪理論」（Bow-wow）已足夠充分。但對真正的哲學家而言，協作理論是唯一觸及或碰觸到待解問題邊緣的理論——這個問題即是：概念如何產生，以及概念如何被表達。
「伴隨的喧囂」（'clamor concomitans'）。

我們僅有一個問題無法回答，即：人類不同行為的伴隨呼喊（clamor concomitans），其意識構成了他們最初的概念，為何恰好是那樣的形式。為何在壓碎時他們發出 MAR，在搬運時發出 VAH，在伸展時發出 TAN，在散播時發出 STAR——這已超出我們的認知範圍。我們所能說的僅是：發出這些聲音的可能性，以及更進一步固定這些聲音的可能性，幾乎是無限的；儘管我們或許可以想像，其中某些聲音似乎帶有某種合理性，但我們很快就會觸及這類推測的極限。

誰不認為字根 VA（吹拂）與我們自身呼吸的聲音之間存在某種相似性？或者，若我們採納擬聲理論，它與風聲又有何異？但若 VA 已是如此，那麼 DHAM（吹氣）與 /SVAS（呼吸）又該如何解釋？在某些情況下，字根的聲音與其所伴隨動作發出的聲音之間存在某種模糊的相似性，這是可以理解的；就此而論，早在柏拉圖《克拉梯樓斯篇》中便已出現的、關於某些字母所謂內在意義的思辨，並非全無價值。或許，若我們能回溯到字根形成的更早階段，他的思辨可能顯得更具根據。然而，我們在此也必須學會滿足於歷史知識所能觸及的範圍；或者，若我們非得將視野極力延伸，便應效法柏拉圖的榜樣，切勿擺出過於嚴肅的面容。
以下是從柏拉圖著作中引用的幾段話，將有助於釐清我的意涵。
『現在，字母 R，』他說道，『在命名者眼中，乃是表達運動的絕佳工具；他經常使用這個字母。』（克拉底魯篇，頁 426）

為此目的：例如，在實際詞語 πζίυ 與 πόρ 中，他以 r 表示運動；亦見於 τρόμος（顫抖）、τραχύς（崎嶇）等詞；此外，在諸如 θραύειν（壓碎）、κρούειν（撞擊）、ἐρείκειν（挫傷）、ἀρύττειν（折斷）、κερματίζειν（粉碎）、ῥυμβεῖν（旋轉）等詞中：他普遍認為所有類型的運動皆可在字母 R 中找到表達，因為，依我之見，他觀察到舌頭在發此字母時最為震動且最不安定，因此他使用此字母來表達運動。

讓我們稍加思索這些論述。要舉出同等數量包含字母 r、且表達靜止而非運動的詞彙，並非難事；例如：*paxis*（肋骨、骨頭、脊柱）、*paxos*（樹籬）、*plyita*（結冰）、*pC(a*（根）、*pvrov*（韁繩）、*pdovvvixt*（加強）、*pGxrrat;*（柱子）等。其次，在柏拉圖所提及的幾個詞彙中，「運動」的含義很容易被證明是派生義而非原始義。舉例而言，若 *K^pixari^iv* 意指「弄碎、切成小片」，這是因為 *Ktpixa* 意指「小片」，而它之所以有此義，是因為它源自 *ndpa*（刮削），最初被用來指稱「碎片」。

但我懷疑，對這些評論進行嚴肅反駁是否合理。它們的用處僅在於顯示這個主題中容許——而且必然容許——多大的自由空間。當現代思辨者從詞根 VA 中看到吹風的模仿時，柏拉圖卻在字母 <p、\j/、<r 與 (（同上，頁 427、434）的聲音中聽見——或者更確切地說，聽出——某種「有風之物」的模仿，因為這些字母的發音伴隨著大量的氣息消耗。當蘇格拉底進一步認為，在發出 d 與 t 時舌頭的閉合與壓迫是束縛與靜止的表達，l 表達平滑，g 表達黏滯，n 表達內在性時，我們不應忘記，赫莫吉尼斯隨即被安排回答：「啊，蘇格拉底，克拉底魯把我弄糊塗了；他說名稱有一種適切性，卻從不解釋那是什麼樣的適切性。」

這是處理這類猜測時應有的正確態度。它們或許偶爾包含幾分真實，但即使如此，那也超出了我們所能掌握的範圍。習俗的力量如此強大，以至於我們全都自以為能在詞根 STA（意為站立或停止）、詞根 MAR（意為摩擦）、詞根 TUD（意為撞擊）中感知到某種恰當性。這種幻想或許有幾分道理，但若我們以更全面的視角審視詞根語音與詞根本義——不僅在雅利安語系中，也在閃語族與圖蘭語族中——我們很快就會發現，事實對我們幻想的證實與否定同樣頻繁。在選擇表達我們行動的語音時，似乎既無必然性，也無絕對自由。即使是那些自認能從中察覺某種合理性的人，也必須承認他們無從檢驗或證明這一點。我們不難理解，在思想伴隨的喧囂中，生存競爭必定極為激烈，儘管我們幾乎已無機會觀察這場競爭。若有學者自認能夠知曉或感受到，為何 SAR 表達了我們對前進的意識，而 VABH 表達了我們對編織的意識，我們無需反駁他們，但我們可以輕易證明，在其他語系中，相同的語音具有截然不同的意義。 378 第十四講
意義。以芬蘭－烏戈爾語系中的詞根 SAR 為例，其含義為：(1) 萌發、湧出、升起；修長、纖細、筆直；
(2) 攪動、喚醒、忙碌；
(3) 摩擦、擦拭、磨利、剪除；
(4) 攪動、混合；使之斑駁、研磨、玷汙；
(5) 推開、擠壓、縮窄、折斷、劈裂、傷害；受苦、受壓迫、萎縮、死亡；
(6) 說話、敘述。

雖然芬蘭語文學的最高權威唐納教授（Professor Donner）將所有這些意義都視為同一個核心概念的變體，但他很可能願意承認，諸如「說話」或「敘述」這類意義可能源自一個獨立的源頭，而與「萌芽」、「冒泡」、「攪動」等概念毫無共通之處；但其他學者則可能堅持認為，「牙牙學語」不過是「冒泡」的變體，而「噴湧」則是「萌芽」的變體。在語言研究中，若說有哪個領域必須容許個人傾向與特質有極大的發揮空間，那便是此處了。我們或許能夠形成一個概括性的概念，瞭解我們所謂的詞根如何在無數可能性的衝突中倖存下來，但我們永遠無法從那些經由對真實語言或語系進行分析而發現的歷史詞根之特性中，發現任何類似於必然性的東西。

語言科學的興起，為宗教學提供了最為關鍵的方法論類比。正如比較語法學透過追溯詞根與屈折變化的共同源頭，得以重建雅利安、閃語族與圖蘭語族諸語言之親屬關係，比較神學與比較神話學亦須遵循相似路徑，方能揭示各大宗教傳統中潛藏的共通結構。穆勒曾言，神話在本質上是「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）——當原始人用以指稱自然現象的隱喻性詞彙逐漸喪失其原初語境，後世便將其誤解為關於神靈與英雄的具體敘事。此一洞見，實為理解宗教觀念之生成與變遷的關鍵鑰匙。
但這已不再是一種理論或單純的夢想¹。¹ 見多納《芬蘭－烏戈爾語比較詞典》第二卷，第1頁。

但一個簡單的事實是：我們所有的詞語都源自這些概念性詞根，而且全部或幾乎全部詞語最初都意指某些原始的人類行為。這些是事實，我們唯一需要補充的，是解釋為何語言應從此類詞根出發，而非從模仿自然聲響開始。我並未像其他學者那樣，完全否定任何詞語可能源自模仿聲音。畢竟，「cuckoo」（杜鵑）是一個詞，儘管或許並非在最嚴格的意義上。「To hush」（使安靜）是一個已演變為表達概念的詞。產生這類詞語的相同過程，就我們所知，本可推展得更遠。但事實仍然是它並未如此，而我們需要解釋的，不是語言可能成為什麼，而是它實際是什麼。表達行為的詞根之所以成為語言真正的源頭，當我們考慮到最早可能——或者我該說，最早無可避免的概念——最自然且最有利的產生條件，莫過於來自我們對自身重複行為的意識時，便變得可以理解。甚至人類的身體構造——擁有兩臂、兩腿、兩眼、兩耳——也會幫助他理解「二」為「一」；而「二元」（Dyad）正是我們所謂概念思維的開端。接著便會是對自身重複且持續行為的意識，而如果這類行為——特別是在共同進行時——伴隨著自然的聲響，也就是因此能被許多人理解的聲響，那麼這場戰役便已獲勝。人類同時知道了擁有概念以及概念的可理解標誌是什麼。其餘一切，正如我們從 380 第十四講 所知的那樣。
語言史的發展，自然會隨之而來。
我們可以從語言的最初階段清楚看到其歷史發展的全貌，尤其是在雅利安語言中，因為這些語言已被最仔細地分析過。但閃語族也向我們訴說著同樣的故事，只不過在這裡，三字母詞根的構成往往阻礙我們觀察詞根與詞根概念發展的最早期階段。三字母詞根最初必定是雙字母或單音節的，儘管在某些情況下可以證明這一點，但尚無法在所有情況中完全確定。對此，我們必須等待來自最古老巴比倫銘文的新線索。
隨著芬諾‧烏戈爾語系的研究在近期取得了長足進展。唐納教授在其《芬諾‧烏戈爾語系比較詞典》中，正為人類語言的這個分支進行著他人對雅利安語系與閃語族分析所做的工作。此處的詞根數量似乎比梵語或希伯來語更少，因此後續意義的衍生與分岔便顯得格外具有啟發性。
其他語系或語言類別至今僅獲得部分成功的分析，然而，只要有所開端，結果始終一致。我們可將其視為一條基本原則，為世界各地所有語言研究者所公認：最初存在著詞根與根本概念，而人類的言語便是從頭到尾以這些詞根建構起來的。
有些語言，例如古漢語，其詞語與詞根至少在表面上是一致的，一切皆為具體質料，尚無任何純粹形式可言。在這樣的語言中，無論其年代多麼久遠，我們再次獲得一個具體的證據，證實我們從分析梵語、希伯來語等更發達語言時為自己所建立的理論：亦即，語言始於詞根，思想始於概念，而兩者本為一體。

我們的概念世界，如同感官世界一般，對每個人而言，都帶有某種個人的色彩。我們用以建構這個世界的材料，並非由我們自己創造；它們是透過語言這項遺產，由過去世代傳遞給我們的。然而，我們每個人都在某種程度上，以自己的方式組合這些材料，賦予它們新的形狀與色調，並將它們融入我們自身存在的結構之中。因此，要完全理解任何一套宗教或哲學體系，我們不僅必須追溯其概念所由來的語言根源，還必須盡可能地重現那些最初孕育並塑造了這些概念的思維狀態。這正是比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的任務，它們是宗教學（the science of religion）這門更廣泛科學的兩大分支。
當人類心智一旦抵達概念階段，戰役便已獲勝，進入理念世界的大門已然敞開。隨著第一個真實詞語的誕生，一個新的世界被創造出來——思想的世界，我們真正的家園。

一旦我們認識到，思想在其真正意義上始終是概念性的，而每個詞語都源自一個概念性的詞根，那麼當我們被告知，詞語既然是概念性的，就永遠無法代表單一的知覺對象時，我們便不再感到驚訝。感官世界中不可能有任何事物能對應於像「狗」、「樹」、「蘋果」、「桌子」這類簡單的詞語，更不用說「顏色」、「美德」、「善」以及所有其他詞語了，因為它們全都是概念性的。我們永遠不可能期望看到一隻狗、一棵樹、一顆蘋果或一張椅子。「狗」意指從靈提到西班牙獵犬的每一種狗；「樹」意指從橡樹到櫻桃樹的每一種樹；「蘋果」意指從鳳梨到斑皮蘋果的每一種蘋果；「椅子」意指從御座到教授講座的每一種椅子。人們時常想像，他們能夠透過剔除每隻個別狗或每種狗的特殊之處，來形成一個關於狗的普遍意象。讓他們試試看吧。
高爾頓先生曾針對人臉做過一項實驗：先拍攝一隻靈緹犬，再疊上一隻西班牙獵犬，接著是一隻聖伯納犬，然後是一隻蘇格蘭㹴，如此反覆，直到所有犬種都疊加完畢。屆時他們便會明白，最終得出的會是何等籠統的形象，而他們也將強烈抗拒在腦中容納這類怪物。

在此，柏克萊亦扮演了最果斷的先驅角色。他指出，任何人根本不可能為自己形成一個三角形的普遍意象，因為這樣的意象必須同時是直角三角形、鈍角三角形、銳角三角形、等邊三角形、等腰三角形與不等邊三角形。這是不可能的，然而要形成任何一個特定三角形的意象，並指出所有三角形共有的某項特徵——即它們皆擁有三個角——從而為三角形創造一個名稱，同時形成一個概念，這卻是完全可行的。柏克萊對現代概念所描述的這種心智過程，若我們審視語言的紀錄，便可觀察到它適用於所有概念，甚至是最原始的概念。人類在較小或較大數量的樹木中——在它們尚未成為他眼中的「樹」之前——發現了某種令他感興趣、且為它們所共有的東西。對原始人而言，樹木因各種理由而有趣，而它們本可因其中任何一個理由而被命名。然而，就實用目的而言，樹木對原始的語言創造者特別有趣，因為它們可以被劈成兩半、切割、塑造成木塊與木板、軸桿與船隻。因此，從詞根 *dar*（撕裂）出發，他們稱樹木為 *dru* 或 *daru*，字面意為「可被劈開、撕裂或切割成碎片的東西」。
從同一詞根，他們也稱皮膚為 βύρσα（býrsa），因為它是被剝下來的；稱袋子為 βορός（borós），因為它是由皮革製成的（梵文 dŕti）；稱長矛為 βορύ（borý），因為它是一棵被砍伐、塑形並刨平的樹。
一旦這類詞語被賦予，便會衍生出無數分支。高盧人稱其祭司為 *Druides*，愛爾蘭語作 *drui*，字面意為「橡樹林之人」。希臘人稱森林樹木的精靈為 *Dryades*；而印度人則稱一個木頭般的人，或一顆木頭般——如我們所說——頑石般的心為 *daruṇa*，意即殘酷。
這個道理不僅適用於「樹」這一個詞，也適用於所有詞語。它們皆源於詞根，皆屬概念，皆表達眾多事物所共有的某種特質——因此，這種特質可以被思考、被言說，卻永遠無法作為單一且真實的對象，被我們的感官所感知。

如果我們是以詞語、且僅以詞語來思考，那麼世界上還有什麼——我現在不單說「更奇妙」，而是說——比我們的詞語更重大、更嚴肅、更主導我們一切理智工作的呢？倘若如此，我們又何須驚訝宗教也將其最深層的根源紮根於語言，甚至若無語言便完全無法設想呢？常有人說「神名即神明」（numina are nomina），而如果我們迄今的論證脈絡一貫正確，我們不僅會接受這個說法，更能理解其真義。試著想像沒有名字的宙斯（Zeus）或希拉（Hera），你會發現根本無從想像。但我們切勿因此就說宙斯與希拉只是「空名」。「只是空名」這個說法，是最令人反感且自相矛盾的表述之一¹。 ¹ 希伯特講座（Hibbert Lectures），約翰‧里斯（John Rhys），頁 221。

在整個哲學辭典中，沒有比這更矛盾的表達了。根本不存在所謂的純粹名稱，正如不存在純粹概念一樣。無論是宙斯（Zeus）之名，甚至赫拉（Hera）之名，背後都蘊含某種意涵；儘管這些名稱既薄弱又僅具嘗試性，且暴露於神話學的所有危險之中，然而希臘人中最優秀者從未忘記宙斯之名真正指向的對象——或許是無限者（the Infinite），那個隱藏於一切名稱背後、無以名狀的力量。各位都記得埃斯庫羅斯（Aeschylus）在《阿伽門農》（Agamemnon）合唱隊中的詩句——但凡讀過之人，又怎能忘懷呢：——

「宙斯，無論祂是誰，若這是祂所喜悅被呼喚的名字——我便以此名呼喚祂。因為若我真想擺脫思慮的無謂重擔，考驗萬事，我發現除了宙斯以外，無人可以託付。」埃斯庫羅斯已知或已預見我們所欲證明之事：宗教即是對無限者的語言或詮釋。或許並無與菲迪亞斯所描繪、希臘民眾所信仰的宙斯相對應之物。但宙斯絕非僅是一個名號。它不過是諸多名稱之一，希臘人——且我們將看到，不僅希臘人，而是所有雅利安人——藉此試圖掌握有限者背後的無限者，試圖以已知者命名未知者，試圖在自然的面紗之後看見神聖者。
1 《語言科學講演錄》，第二卷，第485頁。
